meta.name: The Co-Founder — master knowledge base
meta.purpose: Single consolidated, deduped, tagged source of truth / portable backup. The live app reads library.json + knowledge.js; this file mirrors all of it PLUS the chat-only historical examples.

items.title: The Mom Test — validate without being lied to

content.summary: People lie to be nice. Don't ask if they like the idea — ask about their actual past behavior and whether they already pay to solve it. 'I'd definitely buy that' is a red flag, not a green light.
content.when_to_use: Any time you're judging demand from conversations — before building, when an idea 'tests well' verbally, or when everyone says they love it but nobody buys.
how_to_run: Talk about THEIR life, not your idea
Ask: when did this last happen? what did you do about it? what did it cost you in time or money?
Ask what they've already tried or paid for
Never pitch; if you mention the idea, you've contaminated the answer
Look for behavior and money already spent, not compliments
content.preconditions: You can reach 5-10 people who actually have the problem.
content.failure_modes: Pitching instead of listening; counting compliments as validation; asking hypothetical 'would you' questions; talking to friends who won't be honest.
content.evidence_note: High — the single most repeated, battle-tested lesson in early-stage building across regions; cheap to run and consistently predictive.
content.regional_notes: In high-politeness cultures (much of East/Southeast Asia, parts of the Middle East), verbal enthusiasm is even less reliable — weight observed behavior and prepayment more heavily.
content.examples: Green flag: 'I built a messy spreadsheet to do this and pay an assistant 2 hours a week.' Red flag: 'That sounds amazing, I'd totally use it.'

items.title: Build for a pain you've had
triggers: this annoyed me so i built it
problem i deal with every day

content.summary: Building for a problem you've personally lived gives you a head start on understanding it, but your own pain is one data point, not proof. Confirm other people feel it the same way before you build.
content.when_to_use: Earliest idea stage, when you're choosing what to work on and you have a problem you experience directly and repeatedly.
how_to_run: Write down the exact moment the problem bites and how often it happens to you
Find 5-10 people who are NOT your friends and clearly share that situation
Ask how they handle it today and what it costs them (time, money, frustration)
Listen for whether they've already cobbled together a workaround or paid for one
Only build once strangers describe the pain in roughly the words you would
content.preconditions: You genuinely live the problem on a recurring basis, not a problem you imagine others have.
content.failure_modes: Assuming your pain is universal when you're an outlier. Falling in love with your own workflow and building a tool only you would tolerate. Skipping outside validation because 'I know this problem cold.' Confusing 'I'd use it' with 'I'd pay for it.' A niche of one is not a market.
content.evidence_note: Common startup folklore ('scratch your own itch'); real logic (faster insight, built-in empathy) but no rigorous evidence founder-as-user predicts success, and clear survivorship bias. Grade medium because the validation discipline it's paired with is sound, not the raw heuristic.
content.regional_notes: If you live in a different market than your target users, your lived experience may not transfer; validate locally.
content.examples: A solo developer keeps losing track of unpaid freelance invoices and builds a tracker for herself. Before more code, she messages eight other freelancers in a community; six describe the same scramble and two already pay for a clunky tool. That overlap, not her annoyance, tells her to continue.

items.title: Win the corner they won't defend
triggers: the big player won't bother with this
too small for them to care
it would cannibalize their main product
they make too much on the old way

content.summary: Pick a position the dominant player could copy in theory but won't in practice, because matching you would hurt their existing money-maker. You grow in the gap their own model forces them to ignore, then expand upward.
content.when_to_use: When a strong incumbent owns the market but their pricing, sales model, or core product creates a blind spot they're structurally unwilling to enter.
how_to_run: Name the incumbent's main profit engine and what protecting it forces them to avoid
Find the segment or approach that engine makes them unwilling (not just slow) to serve
Confirm copying you would visibly damage their existing revenue, so they hesitate
Win that segment completely before they react
Plan the climb upward only once your base is defensible
content.preconditions: There is a clear incumbent with a profitable model that genuinely conflicts with serving your chosen segment. If they could match you with no downside, this does not apply.
content.failure_modes: Mistaking 'they haven't noticed yet' for 'they can't afford to follow' — with no real conflict, they just copy you once you prove the market. Picking a corner so small nobody wants it. Assuming the incumbent is slow when a well-funded one may eat the loss to crush you. Staying stuck in the corner and never climbing.
content.evidence_note: Termed 'Counter-Positioning' by Hamilton Helmer (7 Powers, 2016). Respected, coherent framework from observed cases, but canonical examples carry heavy hindsight/survivorship bias. Grade medium: the mechanism (incumbent's model creates unwillingness) is sound; 'do this and win' is not proven.
content.examples: A founder notices the leading scheduling software charges per-seat enterprise pricing via a sales team, so it ignores one-person businesses. He builds a flat-rate self-serve tool for solo operators. The incumbent could add a cheap tier, but that would undercut the per-seat deals their sales org depends on, so they don't. He owns the solo segment for two years.

items.title: Skip the step that never arrived
triggers: they never had the old system here
everyone has a phone but no bank
build for what people already carry
the old way never reached them
mobile is the only computer they own

content.summary: Where the old infrastructure (banks, landlines, fixed broadband, retail chains) never reached people, don't recreate it. Build on whatever they already have, usually a basic phone or mobile connection, and serve the need directly.
content.when_to_use: When your target users live in a market or segment the established delivery method skipped entirely, leaving an open gap.
how_to_run: Identify the legacy infrastructure your service normally rides on and confirm it's genuinely absent
Map what users DO already have and trust (a phone, mobile money, a messaging app)
Design the offer to need only that, not the missing layer
Test that users can complete the whole task on what they own, with their real connectivity
Watch for the missing infrastructure being an effect of a deeper barrier (regulation, trust, income), not just absence
content.preconditions: The old infrastructure is truly missing or weak for your users AND they have reliable access to the substitute you build on.
content.failure_modes: Assuming 'no old system' means 'open opportunity' when the real reason is low ability/willingness to pay. Designing for smartphones when users have feature phones, or steady data when connectivity is patchy. Importing rich-market assumptions (always-on internet, cards, addresses). Underestimating the trust and behavior-change cost of being people's first-ever version of a service.
content.evidence_note: Well-documented in development economics and emerging-market tech (mobile money). The phenomenon is real; the founder lesson ('skip the old layer and win') is more folklore, since many leapfrog attempts fail on affordability/trust. Grade medium: pattern evidenced, success recipe not.
content.regional_notes: Most relevant in emerging markets and underserved rural/low-income segments. Substitute infrastructure varies sharply by region (mobile-money penetration, smartphone vs feature phone, dominant messaging app), so validate locally.
content.examples: A founder helps small rural shopkeepers manage stock and credit. They have no computers and no bank relationship, but nearly all use a basic smartphone and a popular messaging app daily. Instead of accounting software assuming a PC and bank feed, she runs it through the messaging app they already open hourly.

items.title: Boring and monthly beats flashy and once
triggers: something people pay for every month
i don't want to resell every time
recurring need not a one-time buy
money that comes back each month
an ongoing service not a product sale

content.summary: A product people pay for repeatedly because they keep needing it gives predictable income and lets value compound, where a one-time purchase forces you to find a brand-new buyer every sale. Often the 'boring' recurring need is the better business.
content.when_to_use: When shaping your offer and pricing, especially choosing between a one-off sale and an ongoing relationship, or noticing your idea is a one-time purchase.
how_to_run: Check whether the underlying need actually recurs (monthly/weekly) or is genuinely one-and-done
Shape the offer around the ongoing need so paying again is natural, not a forced rental
Price for the recurring value delivered, not a one-time transaction
Watch early renewal/retention as closely as new sales, because churn quietly kills recurring models
Don't bolt a subscription onto something people only need once
content.preconditions: There is a real, repeating need. Forcing recurring pricing onto a one-time need creates churn and resentment, not stability.
content.failure_modes: Slapping a subscription on a one-time-need product so customers cancel after month one. Obsessing over new sign-ups while ignoring churn. Underpricing because 'it's just monthly.' Choosing recurring in a market where buyers prefer to own outright. Confusing 'recurring billing' with 'recurring value' — only the latter retains.
content.evidence_note: Subscription economics are well-studied in SaaS finance and the predictability advantage is real and measurable. But 'boring recurring beats flashy one-time' is a heuristic, not a law; many transactional businesses are excellent and many subscriptions fail on churn. Grade medium.
content.regional_notes: Willingness to subscribe varies by market/category; some regions strongly prefer one-time or prepaid, and recurring-charge infrastructure may be weak.
content.examples: A founder can sell a one-time website setup for restaurants (new restaurant every sale) or offer ongoing menu updates, hosting, and reservations monthly. He picks the monthly service: less glamorous, but after a year he has steady income from the whole base instead of starting from zero — as long as he keeps them from canceling.

items.title: What job is the customer hiring this for?

content.summary: People don't buy products, they 'hire' them to get a specific job done in their life. Understand the job and you understand why someone would switch to you and what you're really competing against.
content.when_to_use: When you can't explain why anyone would pick you over what they do today, when messaging falls flat, or when you're guessing at features instead of knowing the underlying need.
how_to_run: Find people who recently took action on the problem (bought a competitor, built a workaround, quit trying) and have them walk through the day it happened
Pin down the job: what progress were they trying to make, and what triggered them to act then?
Map what they 'fired' to hire your solution, and what held them back from switching
Write your reason-to-exist as the job, not the features
content.preconditions: You need access to real people who recently faced the problem; speculation about jobs is opinion in disguise.
content.failure_modes: Founders invent a flattering 'job' that justifies the product they already built. The framing is qualitative and easy to twist into confident stories with no evidence. It tells you the need, not whether enough people will pay.
content.evidence_note: Popularized by Clayton Christensen; earlier framed by Tony Ulwick and Bob Moesta. A useful, widely-used lens, but a structured-interview/theory approach, not a tested predictive law. Treat insights as hypotheses to validate. Medium.
content.examples: Building a tool to summarize legal contracts, you interview five people who recently paid to handle a contract and learn they weren't hiring 'a summary' — they were hiring 'confidence to sign without a lawyer.' Decision: reframe the product around a clear risk verdict and test whether people pay for that, before more code.

items.title: What changed to make this possible now
triggers: why hasn't this been done already
this wasn't possible two years ago
people only started doing this lately
the tech just got good enough
is it too early for this

content.summary: Ask what recently changed (a technology, a law, a price drop, a shift in behavior) that makes this idea workable now when it wasn't before. If you can't name a clear, recent change, the idea is probably too early or already too late.
content.when_to_use: At idea stage and when pitching, to pressure-test timing and explain why the opportunity is open now.
how_to_run: Ask: if this is a good idea, why doesn't it already exist or dominate?
Identify the specific recent change — tech capability, regulation, cost curve, or new habit — that unlocked it
Date the change: roughly when, and is the window opening or closing?
If you can't name a concrete change, treat that as a red flag
Sanity-check the change is real and durable, not a temporary blip
content.failure_modes: Inventing a vague 'why now' to justify an idea you love. Mistaking a passing trend for a durable shift. Being genuinely too early — users aren't ready. Being too late — incumbents already captured it. Confusing a 'why now' for you with a 'why now' for the market.
content.evidence_note: Standard VC diligence question and pitch slide. A useful framing discipline, but no evidence that articulating a 'why now' predicts success, and easy to write a convincing post-hoc story. Grade medium: respected as a thinking tool, unproven as a predictor.
content.regional_notes: The enabling change (a regulation, a payment rail, a connectivity threshold) often arrives at different times in different regions, so a 'why now' true in one market may be too early or late in another.
content.examples: A founder wants to turn spoken customer calls into structured notes. Asked why now, she points to a recent drop in cost and jump in accuracy of speech-to-text within the last year or two — the same product would have been too inaccurate and expensive a few years earlier. That datable change is her 'why now.'

items.title: Your unfair, hard-to-copy edge
triggers: what do i bring that others can't
i have access nobody else has
i know this industry from the inside
i can reach these customers easily
what makes me hard to copy
do i have an edge here
why am i the right person

content.summary: Identify what you specifically know, have access to, or can reach that a smart competitor can't easily copy — insider knowledge, a built-in audience, rare skills, trust in a community. Being passionate or hardworking is not an advantage; everyone serious has those.
content.when_to_use: When choosing among ideas, or honestly assessing whether you (versus anyone else) are well-placed to win this opportunity.
how_to_run: List concrete assets: specialized knowledge, relationships, distribution/audience, rare skills, community trust
For each, ask 'could a capable stranger get this in a few months?' — if yes, it's not an edge
Match your real edges against what this opportunity actually rewards
Ruthlessly cut 'I'm passionate' and 'I work hard'
If you find no durable edge, build one first or pick a different opportunity
content.preconditions: You can be honest with yourself; this only works if you don't flatter yourself about advantages you don't have.
content.failure_modes: Counting passion, grit, or generic intelligence as an advantage — they're table stakes. Inventing an edge to justify an idea you already want. Overrating an advantage that's easy to acquire. Underrating quiet but real edges (a community's trust). Believing an edge is permanent. A genuine edge raises odds but doesn't guarantee the opportunity is good.
content.evidence_note: 'Founder-market fit' popularized ~2012 as a companion to product-market fit. Intuitive and widely cited but largely conceptual; no rigorous evidence that measurable founder-market fit predicts outcomes, and fuzzy enough to define after the fact. Graded low and flagged because it invites self-flattering, survivorship-biased storytelling.
content.regional_notes: Local knowledge, language, and on-the-ground relationships are often a real, hard-to-copy edge in a specific region; an edge strong in one market may be worthless in another.
content.examples: A founder considering a tool for physiotherapy clinics realizes her edge isn't enthusiasm — it's three years as a clinic receptionist, knowing exactly how their billing breaks, and being trusted in a 2,000-member group of clinic owners. A generalist could copy features but not that knowledge or warm distribution. She drops the ideas where she has no such edge.

items.title: Dominate one tiny market completely
triggers: pick one type of customer first
be the obvious choice for one group
who exactly is my first customer

content.summary: Pick the smallest, most specific market you can win completely — one tight customer type with a shared, urgent need — and become the obvious choice there before expanding. Trying to serve everyone at once usually means winning no one.
content.when_to_use: When defining your first target customer, especially if tempted to aim at a broad market or many segments at once.
how_to_run: Narrow to one specific customer type who share the same urgent problem and talk to each other
Confirm it's small enough to dominate but real enough to sustain early traction
Make your product unmistakably the best choice for exactly that group
Win it deeply — high usage, referrals, repeatable sales — before widening
Plan expansion into adjacent segments from strength, not in parallel
content.preconditions: There's a definable, reachable segment with a shared urgent need you can plausibly serve better than anyone, and an adjacent path to expand later.
content.failure_modes: Picking a beachhead so tiny it can never grow. Defining the segment too broadly so you can't dominate. Expanding before truly winning the first market, splitting focus. Choosing a segment that doesn't talk to itself, so word of mouth never compounds. Falling so in love with the niche you never expand. A beachhead is a starting point, not a ceiling.
content.evidence_note: Rooted in Geoffrey Moore's Crossing the Chasm (1991); reinforced by later GTM frameworks. One of the most widely taught GTM ideas and coherent, but supporting cases are retrospective and survivorship-biased. Grade medium: strong as a focus discipline, not a guarantee.
content.regional_notes: A beachhead can be geographic — winning one city or region completely before expanding — often practical for first-time founders with limited reach.
content.examples: A founder building project-management software resists aiming at 'all small businesses' and targets only independent wedding planners — a group with a specific workflow who swap recommendations in online groups. Becoming the tool wedding planners assume everyone uses gets dense word of mouth, then she adapts for event planners more broadly.

items.title: Write the launch announcement before you build
triggers: describe the product before building it
does this sound boring on paper

content.summary: Before building anything, write the short announcement you'd publish on launch day plus the uncomfortable questions a real customer would ask. If it reads as boring or you can't answer the hard questions, that's a cheap signal to stop or rethink.
content.when_to_use: At the very start, when you have an idea but haven't committed engineering time. Best when you're tempted to build because the idea feels obvious, or when a team disagrees about what they're making.
how_to_run: Write a one-paragraph 'announcement' as if it shipped: who it's for, the single problem it kills, why they'd switch today. Plain customer language.
Write the FAQ a skeptical buyer would ask: cost, why trust you, the obvious cheaper alternative, what happens when it breaks.
Read it cold a day later. If the announcement is boring or the FAQ has answers you can't honestly give, it isn't ready.
Show it to 3-5 target customers and watch reaction to the paragraph, not your pitch. Indifference is the kill signal.
Only then scope the smallest build that delivers that paragraph.
content.preconditions: You need a clear guess at who the customer is. If you can't name the customer, do customer discovery first.
content.failure_modes: Writing it to sound impressive instead of honest so it always 'passes.' Treating it as a one-time gate instead of revising as you learn. Internal feature jargon hiding that the value is unclear. Polishing the doc for weeks while learning nothing from real people. Confusing a good paragraph with actual demand — it tests clarity, not whether anyone pays.
content.evidence_note: Popularized by Amazon's internal product process; widely taught as a way to kill weak ideas cheaply. Medium: the logic (force clarity before spending) is sound and broadly endorsed, but evidence is practitioner testimony and survivorship-flavored retellings, not controlled studies. Improves clarity; does not prove demand.
content.examples: A solo technical founder wants to build a scheduling tool for clinics. The launch paragraph reads identically to three free tools clinics already ignore, and the hard FAQ ('why switch?') has no good answer. They stop before coding, saving two months, and pivot toward the one thing clinics complained about: no-show reminders.

items.title: Concierge MVP — deliver the outcome by hand first

content.summary: Deliver the result manually for your first users — a spreadsheet, DMs, your own labor — and charge real money. You learn what's truly needed and skip building the wrong software.
content.when_to_use: Before writing significant code, when you're unsure exactly what to build.
how_to_run: Find 1-3 people with the problem now
Deliver the outcome by hand, charging real money
Note every step that's painful and repeated
Automate ONLY those parts, in that order
content.preconditions: The outcome can be delivered manually at small scale.
content.failure_modes: Automating before you understand the job; treating it as a demo instead of charging.
content.evidence_note: High — repeatedly how durable products were discovered; manual delivery surfaces the real requirements cheaply.

items.title: Fake the automation, do it by hand
triggers: fake it before you build it
do it manually behind the scenes
see if people actually want it

content.summary: Put up a polished front-end so users believe the product works automatically, while you quietly do the work by hand. It tests whether people actually want the thing before you build the expensive engine behind it.
content.when_to_use: When the value is clear but the automation is expensive or risky to build, and you want real behavior (not opinions) before committing. Good for technical founders tempted to build the hard backend first.
how_to_run: Build only the front-end the user touches: a form, a chat, a page that looks like a working product.
When a request comes in, fulfill it manually behind the scenes, fast enough that it feels automated.
Measure real actions: did they submit, return, pay, refer. Watch what they do, not what they say.
Track how often the manual work would need automating — this tells you what to build first and whether it's worth it.
Once demand is proven and the manual load hurts, automate the single most painful step, not the whole system.
content.preconditions: You can do the work by hand at small scale, well enough that users don't feel cheated. Don't fake things that are dangerous, regulated, or where a hidden human creates a privacy/safety problem.
content.failure_modes: Faking a step where a hidden human handling user data is unethical or illegal (health, finance, identity). Burning out doing manual work long after demand is proven. Reading 'they tolerated my manual service' as 'they'll love the automated one.' Tiny samples that feel like validation. Letting quality slip so the test measures your hustle, not the product.
content.evidence_note: Well-established in UX and lean-startup practice; distinct from concierge testing (where the user knows a human is involved). Medium: strongly endorsed and useful, but public proof is selected success stories; it tests demand for a manual experience, which doesn't guarantee the automated version retains.
content.examples: A founder thinks small online sellers want AI-written product descriptions. Instead of building the model pipeline, they put up a form 'paste your product, get a description in 1 hour' and write each by hand. Within two weeks ten sellers return repeatedly and two offer to pay — and most requests are one category, so that's where automation should start.

items.title: Ship something a few people love, not a list nobody wanted
triggers: don't build a huge feature list
make a few users happy first
stop adding features nobody asked for
first version that people rave about
focus on one strong use case

content.summary: Instead of shipping the longest feature list, ship the smallest product that a narrow group genuinely love and would miss if it vanished. Depth of love from a few beats shallow indifference from many.
content.when_to_use: When defining your first real release and feeling pressure to match competitors feature-for-feature. Best when you can identify a specific narrow group whose problem you can fully solve.
how_to_run: Pick one narrow user group and the single job they care about most. Say no to every other audience for now.
Define what 'they'd be upset if this disappeared' looks like — that bar, not feature count, is the target.
Build only what's needed to clear that bar, but finish those few things to a genuinely good standard.
Put it in front of that group and watch for unprompted enthusiasm and repeat use, not polite approval.
Expand only after a core of users clearly love it; widen audience or add features one at a time.
content.preconditions: You know who the narrow group is and what 'love' means for them. If you don't yet know the customer or problem, do discovery first — this is a build approach, not a discovery one.
content.failure_modes: Using 'lovable' as an excuse to over-polish and delay shipping. Confusing your own taste for what users love. Picking an audience so narrow it can never grow. Chasing delight on things users don't care about while ignoring a basic broken step. Mistaking early enthusiasm from friendly users for real market love.
content.evidence_note: A popular reframing of 'MVP'; widely repeated but largely opinion and anecdote. Low: the underlying idea (intensity of preference from a focused segment matters) is reasonable, but little rigorous evidence that 'lovable' beats 'viable' as a rule, and 'love' is hard to measure. A useful framing, not a proven law.
content.examples: A founder building a personal-finance app lists 20 features to match incumbents, then cuts to one: automatically flagging the three subscriptions you forgot you're paying for. They make that flow effortless and a little delightful, and a small group start telling friends specifically about it — a real wedge before adding anything else.

items.title: Watch the real thing happen where it happens
triggers: see it with your own eyes

content.summary: Don't rely only on dashboards, summaries, or what people tell you in meetings. Go to where the work or buying actually happens and watch it directly, because real behavior is usually different from the report.
content.when_to_use: When a metric looks surprising, a feature isn't used as expected, support keeps getting the same complaint, or you're about to decide based on second-hand summaries. Especially valuable for technical founders who default to data over people.
how_to_run: Identify the exact spot where the real action happens: a user doing the task, a customer deciding to buy, a support call, an onboarding.
Go there in person or watch live (screen-share, recording). Don't interrupt or coach.
Note the gap between the report/your assumption and what you saw — especially workarounds and friction nobody mentions.
Ask 'why' about the surprising moments, digging past the first explanation.
Bring the specific observation back into the decision; change what you build based on real behavior.
content.preconditions: You need access to real users or the real workflow. If you can't observe directly, get the closest proxy (recordings, session replays) rather than relying purely on aggregate numbers.
content.failure_modes: Watching once and over-generalizing from a single case. Letting your presence change behavior. Cherry-picking the observation that confirms what you believed. Replacing all quantitative data with anecdote. Observing politely and never asking the hard 'why.' Confusing 'I saw one user struggle' with 'this is the top priority' without checking frequency.
content.evidence_note: Rooted in lean/operations practice (direct observation) and user-research methods that stress watching behavior over self-report. Medium: observation reliably surfaces problems surveys/dashboards miss (well supported in UX research); the weakness is sample size and observer bias, so it informs rather than proves.
content.examples: A founder sees 60% of users drop off at setup and assumes the form is too long. Watching five real users, the form is fine — they abandon because an earlier email never arrives, so they're stuck waiting for a code. The dashboard blamed the form; watching revealed email delivery.

items.title: Build for how your customers actually pay and behave
triggers: most users have no credit card
what works there won't work here
adapt to how people really buy

content.summary: Don't import a playbook built for rich, high-trust, card-and-broadband markets. Design around how your actual customers pay, move, and behave, which may mean cash, mobile money, low bandwidth, and offline trust.
content.when_to_use: When building in or for a market whose payment, connectivity, logistics, or trust conditions differ from where most startup advice originates. Especially when tempted to clone a successful foreign app step-for-step.
how_to_run: List the assumptions baked into the playbook you're copying: cards, fast internet, reliable address/delivery, high baseline trust.
Check each against your real customers: how they actually pay, their connection, how goods reach them, whether they trust paying before receiving.
Redesign the friction points around reality: cash-on-delivery, mobile wallets, lightweight/offline flows, local-language trust signals.
Test the riskiest local assumption first (often payment or last-mile) with a small real run before scaling.
Build operations around the messy parts (returns, failed deliveries, collections) where imported models quietly break.
content.preconditions: You need genuine local knowledge, ideally from living it or talking to many real customers, not assumptions about 'developing markets' in general. Conditions vary enormously even within one country.
content.failure_modes: Treating a whole region as uniform. Under-pricing the operational cost of cash-on-delivery (high return rates, cash handling, fraud). Over-engineering for low-end conditions a segment has outgrown. Copying a 'localized' competitor without checking if their model makes money. Assuming low trust is permanent. Romanticizing local quirks instead of solving the underlying need.
content.evidence_note: Supported by market research on emerging-market commerce: low card penetration, large unbanked populations, strong cash-on-delivery preference. Medium: the pattern is well-documented across regions; the limit is that conditions shift fast and vary locally, so specific tactics age and don't transfer cleanly.
content.regional_notes: Most relevant in markets with low card penetration, large unbanked populations, unreliable addressing, or low baseline trust in prepayment — common across much of South Asia, Africa, Southeast Asia, and parts of Latin America. Even within one country, urban and rural realities differ.
content.examples: A founder copies a card-first subscription-box model that thrives abroad. Locally almost no target customers hold credit cards and few trust paying before they see the goods. After near-zero signups, they switch to cash-on-delivery and a mobile wallet, and orders come — but they also must handle the high share of customers who refuse delivery at the door.

items.title: When fear of getting scammed is the real blocker, trust is the product
triggers: buyers are scared of getting scammed

content.summary: If people want what you offer but won't transact because they fear scams or bad quality, the missing trust IS the product, not a feature you add later. Providing it (escrow, guarantees, protection) can unlock the market, but it's capital- and operations-heavy and hard to reverse.
content.when_to_use: When you've confirmed real demand on both sides but deals stall because someone must 'go first' and fears being cheated. The core objection is 'how do I know I won't get scammed,' not price or awareness.
how_to_run: Confirm trust is actually the blocker: talk to people who almost transacted and didn't.
Start with the lightest trust mechanism that could work — clear guarantees, verification, reviews, manual dispute handling — before heavy financial infrastructure.
If money must be held (escrow), understand the legal, regulatory, and capital obligations upfront; holding others' funds is heavily regulated.
Pilot on a small set of transactions you can personally guarantee or refund, and measure whether removing the fear unlocks deals.
Only scale the capital/ops-heavy machinery once you've proven trust is the bottleneck and solving it converts.
content.preconditions: Validate two-sided demand first; a trust layer for a market that doesn't exist is just expensive infrastructure. Understand the legal/regulatory rules for handling money or guarantees in each market.
content.failure_modes: Holding customer funds without licenses, capital, or compliance — can be illegal and hard to unwind. Underpricing guarantees and refunds; fraud/disputes can eat the business. Building escrow before proving anyone wanted the transaction. Assuming trust is the blocker when the real issue is price or weak demand. Trust mechanisms becoming a fraud target. Once you've promised protection you can't quietly remove it — why it's one-way.
content.evidence_note: Marketplace and payment practitioners consistently cite trust (fear of non-payment or bad quality) as a core two-sided-marketplace blocker, with escrow/buyer protection as common solutions. Medium: mechanism well-attested and intuitive, but public evidence leans on vendor case studies, and the capital/regulatory burden means it works only when trust is genuinely the binding constraint.
content.regional_notes: The bar for 'enough trust' varies by market. Where institutional trust is weak and prepayment uncommon, this layer can be the whole reason a marketplace works — but it collides with local financial regulation, which can require licenses to hold or move funds.
content.examples: A founder runs a marketplace for custom furniture. Both sides are interested but deals die: buyers won't pay upfront for something they can't inspect, workshops won't start without a deposit. They pilot holding the buyer's payment and releasing on delivery, refunding personally on the first disputes. Orders close — but they learn legally holding customer money requires registration they hadn't set up, forcing a pause to get compliant.

items.title: Charge from day one — money is the only real validation

content.summary: A free user is not a customer. Money is the only validation that survives contact with reality. Ask for the sale early — willingness to pay tells you what 'likes' never will.
content.when_to_use: As soon as you have something that delivers value, even manually; whenever you're tempted to 'just get users first'.
how_to_run: Name a real price out loud
Ask for payment or a deposit now, not 'later'
Treat a 'yes, but not yet' as a no and dig into why
Use the no's: 'what would have to be true for this to be a yes?'
content.failure_modes: Giving it away to avoid the scary ask; chasing signups/likes (vanity) instead of revenue; assuming you'll 'monetize later'.
content.evidence_note: High — prepayment/early revenue is the most reliable early signal across regions and sectors.

items.title: Founder-led sales — sell the first 10-50 yourself

content.summary: Sell the first ~10-50 customers personally, by hand. Don't hire salespeople before you've cracked the pitch yourself — you can't delegate a sale you haven't figured out.
how_to_run: List 20 specific people who have the problem
Reach out one-to-one, not a broadcast
Do the calls/demos yourself; note every objection
Refine the pitch until it converts before hiring anyone
content.failure_modes: Hiring a salesperson to escape the discomfort of selling; broadcasting instead of one-to-one; not logging objections.
content.evidence_note: High — near-universal in B2B and most early B2C; the founder's job at this stage IS sales.

items.title: Sell to one specific buyer first
triggers: who should I sell to first

content.summary: Sell to one specific kind of person before 'everyone'; a sharp message to a narrow buyer beats a vague one to all.
content.when_to_use: When you are pre-launch or in your first months, struggling to get attention, or when your pitch tries to appeal to too many people at once and lands with none.
how_to_run: Write the single most specific person who has the problem most painfully today (role, situation, what they use now).
Rewrite your one-line pitch so it names that person's exact situation, not a general benefit.
Find 10-20 of exactly that person and talk to or sell to only them for a fixed period.
Track whether the sharper message gets more replies, calls, or sales than the broad one.
Only widen to adjacent buyers once this narrow group is buying and referring.
content.preconditions: You can actually reach the narrow group (a findable channel, list, or community). Narrowing to a buyer you can't locate just makes you invisible.
content.failure_modes: Picking a niche so tiny it can never grow. Confusing a narrow MESSAGE with a permanently narrow product. Choosing by who's easiest to reach rather than who hurts most. Narrowing on a demographic ('women 25-34') instead of a situation/problem. Switching niches every few weeks before any one has a fair test.
content.evidence_note: Strong logical and practitioner support: focused messaging reliably lifts response rates and a concentrated first segment is standard early advice. Medium, not high, because most public 'proof' is survivorship-biased retellings and there are no clean controlled trials isolating narrowing as the cause. The mechanism (relevance raises conversion) is well-established in direct response.
content.regional_notes: In smaller or non-English markets the reachable niche may be smaller, so balance 'narrow' against having enough buyers; a tight vertical can work better than a tight demographic.
content.examples: A solo founder with a scheduling tool 'for any small business' gets ignored. They rewrite it as 'for independent physiotherapists who lose hours rebooking no-shows,' join two forums where those people gather, and get their first paying users within weeks.

items.title: How you frame it decides who buys
triggers: how do I describe my product
how to explain what we do
who is the competition in their mind

content.summary: How you frame the product changes who buys and what they'll pay; the reference point you set ('the cheap one,' 'the X for Y') decides how people judge you.
content.when_to_use: When prospects 'don't get it,' compare you to the wrong thing, or balk at price. When deciding what category to claim, what to compare against, or how to name the product.
how_to_run: Write what customers currently think you are and what they compare you to (ask them, don't guess).
List the few attributes your best customers actually value, and pick the frame that makes those obviously matter.
Choose a reference point on purpose: the thing you want to be measured against sets price and quality expectations.
Test the new framing on fresh prospects and watch whether the 'what is this / why that price' friction drops.
Make website, pitch, and pricing page all tell the same single story.
content.preconditions: You need real signal on how current customers describe and value the product. Positioning invented in a vacuum tends to be wishful.
content.failure_modes: Calling yourself 'the cheapest' when buyers want reliability, repelling them and attracting churning bargain-hunters. Claiming a frame the product can't deliver. Positioning against a giant and inviting a comparison you lose. Changing the frame so often no reputation accumulates. Clever framing the buyer doesn't understand.
content.evidence_note: Well-supported in marketing theory (reference points and category framing shift willingness-to-pay) and consistent with anchoring research. Medium because outcomes are context-dependent and most business 'case studies' are after-the-fact; framing helps but rarely rescues a weak product.
content.regional_notes: Category language and status cues differ by market; a frame that signals 'premium' in one country can read as 'overpriced' or unfamiliar in another. Translate the meaning, not just the words.
content.examples: A founder sells a simple invoicing tool as 'the cheapest invoicing app,' attracting price-sensitive users who cancel after a month. Reframing it as 'invoicing built for freelancers who hate accounting' draws people who value the simplicity and stay, at the same price.

items.title: Take the buyer's risk off the table

content.summary: If people fear buying from an unknown, move the risk onto yourself: pay-after-delivery, a real money-back guarantee, or a free trial that produces an actual outcome.
content.when_to_use: When prospects show interest but stall at the moment of payment, especially when you're new, unknown, or selling something they can't easily evaluate up front.
how_to_run: Identify the specific fear stopping the purchase (wasted money, won't work for me, hard to undo).
Pick the smallest risk-reversal that directly answers that fear, not a generic discount.
Make the guarantee concrete and easy to claim, and tie the trial to a real result the buyer can see.
Track conversion before and after, and track how many actually invoke the refund or churn after the trial.
Keep it only if extra sales outweigh refunds, abuse, and support cost.
content.preconditions: Your product must reliably deliver the promised outcome, and you must be able to absorb refunds or non-payment without going under. Risk reversal on a product that doesn't work just accelerates losses.
content.failure_modes: Money-back on a weak product, bleeding refunds. A 'free trial' too short or clunky to reach the 'aha,' converting no one. Attracting freebie-seekers who never intended to pay. Pay-after-delivery to customers who won't pay, creating collections nightmares. Hiding conditions in fine print, destroying the trust you wanted.
content.evidence_note: Solid grounding in loss-aversion research and direct-response practice, where credible guarantees and outcome-based trials reliably lift conversion. Medium because the net effect depends on product quality and refund/abuse rates; the lever can backfire and there's no universal success rate.
content.regional_notes: Refund and consumer-protection laws differ sharply by country (some mandate cooling-off/return rights), and norms around paying upfront vs on delivery vary; check what's legally required and culturally expected.
content.examples: A founder selling a paid onboarding service finds prospects interested but nervous. They switch to 'you only pay if your team is fully set up in two weeks.' Sign-ups jump, almost everyone pays because the service genuinely delivers, and the few who don't cost little.

items.title: Make people line up and invite friends
triggers: invite to move up the list
will a waitlist work for me

content.summary: Make people line up and invite friends to move up — but only when real demand already exists; the mechanic amplifies demand, it can't create it.
content.when_to_use: When you have evidence people genuinely want the thing (unprompted sign-ups, organic sharing) and you want to organize that demand, stagger access, and lower acquisition cost. An amplifier, not a substitute for product-market fit.
how_to_run: Confirm underlying pull first: are people asking for it, sharing it, or signing up without incentives?
Build a simple list with an honest reason to share (earlier access, a real perk).
Watch your real referral rate: how many new sign-ups each existing one brings; below ~1 it won't snowball.
Convert the list — track how many actually pay or use the product, not just the headline count.
Drop the mechanic if sign-ups are vanity (lots of emails, no conversions).
content.preconditions: Genuine pre-existing demand and a product worth waiting for. Without that, a referral loop just inflates a list of people who never convert.
content.failure_modes: Chasing a big sign-up number that never becomes paying users. Assuming you'll go viral; sustained referral rates above 1 are rare. Paying for incentives that attract reward-hunters. Manufacturing artificial scarcity that annoys people. A long waitlist launched into too slowly, letting interest go cold.
content.evidence_note: Graded LOW deliberately. Famous 'waitlist wins' are textbook survivorship bias; most referral waitlists fizzle. Reported pre-launch viral coefficients typically sit ~0.2-0.6 (below the >1 needed to self-sustain) per growth-tool vendors who profit from selling waitlist tools, so treat cautiously. The mechanic can lower CAC where demand already exists; it does not generate demand.
content.regional_notes: Referral incentives intersect with promotion/lottery and data-protection rules in some jurisdictions (e.g. consent for emailing referred contacts); 'share to unlock' norms vary by platform and region.
content.examples: A founder with a developer tool notices people already sharing their landing page in chat groups unprompted. They add 'invite two teammates to get access a week early,' organizing that existing enthusiasm into staggered low-cost sign-ups — and a healthy share convert because the demand was real first.

items.title: Beat a percentage fee with a flat one
triggers: win on price for large accounts
should I charge a flat rate
my costs don't scale with customer size

content.summary: If the leader's percentage fee punishes high-volume customers, charge a low flat fee and win those accounts — but only if your own costs don't scale with their volume.
content.when_to_use: When an incumbent charges a percentage (of revenue, transactions, spend, headcount) and their biggest customers feel gouged because their bill grows without matching extra value.
how_to_run: Find the customers for whom the incumbent's percentage fee is most painful (high volume, thin extra benefit).
Confirm your own cost to serve them stays roughly flat as volume grows; if your costs scale too, a flat fee bankrupts you.
Set a flat fee clearly below what those customers pay today, with margin built in at realistic volumes.
Aim your message at the pain ('stop paying more just because you grew') to the accounts that feel it most.
Watch whether you attract the high-volume accounts you wanted, not just tiny ones for whom flat is expensive.
content.preconditions: Your marginal cost to serve must stay near-flat as a customer's volume rises. You also need enough large accounts to make the volume play worthwhile.
content.failure_modes: Discovering your costs DO scale (servers, payments, support), so big customers become loss-makers. Winning only small accounts for whom flat is a bad deal. Triggering the incumbent to add a flat tier. Setting the fee so low there's no margin at scale. Adverse selection where only the heaviest, costliest users switch.
content.evidence_note: Grounded in standard pricing/cost-structure analysis: a flat fee wins high-volume buyers precisely when seller marginal cost is low and decoupled from buyer volume. Medium because success is contingent on your real cost curve and competitive response, and clean public evidence is scarce; the logic is sound but unforgiving if the cost assumption is wrong.
content.regional_notes: In payments, marketplaces, and financial services, fee structures may be constrained by regulation or interchange rules; what you can charge and how you disclose it varies by jurisdiction.
content.examples: A founder competes with a payments tool taking 2% of every transaction. Their own cost per customer is basically fixed hosting. They offer a flat monthly fee that's far cheaper for high-volume merchants, and deliberately court exactly those merchants — who overpay most under the percentage model.

items.title: Free or below-cost to grab the market

content.summary: Going free or below-cost to grab a market only works with deep capital AND a proven, specific way to make money later; without both it's just burning cash.
content.when_to_use: Rarely, and almost never for a first-time, capital-light founder. Only consider it with genuinely deep funding, a clear evidenced later-monetization mechanism, and strong reasons the market tips to a single winner. Default to NOT doing this.
how_to_run: Be honest about funding: can you sustain losses for years, not months, without a forced shutdown?
Define exactly how you'll make money later and show evidence it converts, before subsidizing anything.
Confirm winners actually lock in here (network effects, switching costs); if customers leave when the subsidy ends, you've bought nothing.
Run a small subsidized test and measure whether subsidized users ever become profitable, with real numbers.
Set a kill-line in advance: the loss rate or runway point at which you stop, and honor it.
content.preconditions: BOTH deep, patient capital AND a proven path to monetize the customers you acquire. Plus a credible reason the market consolidates to a winner. Missing any one turns this into terminal cash burn.
content.failure_modes: Running out of money before monetization arrives. Acquiring users who only want the free thing and vanish when you charge. A deeper-pocketed competitor outlasting you. Customers anchoring on 'free' so you can never raise price. Margin recovery that never materializes. Mistaking subsidized growth for real demand.
content.evidence_note: Graded LOW and flagged WARN. Subsidize-to-dominate is real in economics but the famous successes are extreme survivorship bias backed by capital almost no founder has; for everyone else it's a leading cause of death. Practitioner/economic sources agree it only pays when unit economics and a measured path to margin recovery are proven first — and competitors can simply match prices.
content.regional_notes: Below-cost or predatory pricing is illegal in some jurisdictions (anti-dumping / predatory-pricing laws); sustained below-cost selling can draw regulatory or competitor legal action depending on market and size.
content.examples: A solo founder with limited savings offers the product entirely free to 'win the market fast.' Thousands sign up, but when cash runs low and they introduce a price, almost everyone leaves — the free users were never going to pay, and a better-funded rival simply keeps its version free.

items.title: Stay lean: hire builders, not managers, until there's a machine to manage
triggers: do I need a VP yet

content.summary: Don't hire a fancy manager before there's a machine to manage; hire builders to clear real bottlenecks, and watch your burn.
content.when_to_use: When tempted to bring on a senior 'leader' (head of X, VP, COO) early, or when you feel busy and assume the fix is more headcount.
how_to_run: Write what work is actually undone now and who'd do it day one — if the honest answer is 'they'd manage people I don't have yet,' it's too early.
Prefer a hands-on builder over a coordinator until there's repeatable work and 3+ people to manage.
Translate the hire into burn: add fully-loaded cost to monthly spend and recompute runway before committing.
Set a concrete 90-day output; if you can't name one, it's a manager-in-disguise.
Revisit each few hires — the layer is worth it once you genuinely can't hold coordination in your own head.
content.preconditions: You have some validated demand or product, so you know which builders move the needle. Pre-traction this often means hiring no one.
content.failure_modes: Hiring a pedigreed exec who needs a team and process that don't exist, creating overhead not output. The manager builds reporting structure no one needs. Founder feels 'senior' progress while burn climbs and work still isn't done. The expensive hire leaves within a year — a costly one-way reversal.
content.evidence_note: Widely repeated in startup-hiring guidance and supported by data that ~46% of newly hired executives fail within 18 months, often on fit (Leadership IQ). The 'lean until there's a machine' framing is sound operator practice but heuristic, not a controlled finding — medium.
content.regional_notes: In grant/subsidy-heavy ecosystems, founders sometimes hire managers to 'look fundable'; resist optics-driven hires. Where senior talent only moves for a title, weigh that against the burn math.
content.examples: A solo technical founder with a live product and a few paying customers almost hires a 'Head of Operations.' There are no operations to head — what's undone is shipping features and answering support. They hire a second engineer, ship faster, and revisit the ops role only once the team reaches five.

items.title: Hire to remove the real constraint, not the chores you dislike
triggers: what role should I hire first

content.summary: Hire to remove the constraint actually capping growth, not just the tasks you personally dislike.
content.when_to_use: When deciding which role to open next, or when swamped and assuming any hire helps.
how_to_run: Name the one thing that, if it moved, would most increase growth or survival — that's the constraint.
Check: is high-value work not happening because you're stuck on low-value work? That's being the bottleneck, vs merely busy.
Quantify the cost of the constraint ('I lose ~$X/month because I can't handle more than Y') — make it a math decision.
Define the role around relieving that constraint; if it only offloads disliked tasks but the constraint stays, wrong hire.
After hiring, confirm the constraint actually moved — if not, you misdiagnosed it.
content.preconditions: You can observe what's actually capping growth. Very early, the constraint is usually finding demand, not headcount.
content.failure_modes: Hiring to escape disliked chores while the true constraint (you can't sell, the product's too slow) goes untouched. Offloading work that was never the bottleneck, so growth doesn't change but burn does. Mistaking 'busy' for 'blocked' and adding people to a process that needs simplifying. Constraint shifts after the hire and you keep optimizing the old one.
content.evidence_note: Strongly aligned with theory-of-constraints reasoning and consistent first-hire advice that the trigger should be a measurable binding bottleneck, not feeling busy. Logically robust and well-supported in practice, but a diagnostic principle rather than an empirically validated rule — medium.
content.examples: A founder dreads bookkeeping and almost hires a part-time finance person. But sales are flat because nobody follows up on inbound leads — that's the real constraint. They hire for sales follow-up instead; revenue rises, and bookkeeping (a chore, not a constraint) gets handled with a cheap tool later.

items.title: Trial senior and leadership hires with paid work before committing
triggers: should I hire this senior person
how to de-risk a big hire
how to vet a leadership hire

content.summary: Senior and leadership hires are hard-to-reverse, one-way doors; run a paid contract or trial project before committing full-time.
content.when_to_use: Before any senior, expensive, or leadership hire — especially one you'd struggle to undo without real damage to morale, cash, or trust.
how_to_run: Scope a real, paid piece of work (a contract, trial sprint, or defined project) that mirrors the actual job.
Agree in writing what 'good' looks like and that this is a mutual trial, not a guaranteed offer.
Watch how they work, communicate, and collaborate under real conditions — not just whether they finish.
Decide on observed evidence, not pedigree or interview charm; convert to full-time only if the trial clearly worked.
Keep it short and bounded so a 'no' is cheap and graceful for both sides.
content.preconditions: There's real, fundable work to hand over and budget to pay for it; the candidate is willing and legally able to contract.
content.failure_modes: Skipping the trial because the candidate 'interviewed brilliantly,' then managing a mis-hire for months. Making it unpaid or fake, which repels strong candidates and gives a false read. Trialing for likability instead of the job. Using it to extract cheap labor, poisoning trust. For genuinely senior leaders, no two-week sprint fully captures judgment over time.
content.evidence_note: Failed senior hires are expensive — recruiter fees alone can be six figures, and total cost of a leadership mis-hire is widely estimated in the millions once stalled strategy and lost trust count. Paid trials and contract-to-hire are well-regarded de-risking tactics, though effectiveness isn't rigorously measured — medium.
content.regional_notes: Many countries have strong notice/severance obligations, making the one-way door harder to reverse — strengthening the case for a trial. But local labor law may treat a paid trial as employment; check before structuring one. Candidates in stable jobs may be unable to take a contract trial, so adapt.
content.examples: A founder wants a first head of engineering. Instead of a direct full-time offer, they pay for a two-week scoped project on a real roadmap item. The work is fine but the person ignores the small team and won't explain decisions — exactly the failure that would be catastrophic at this stage. They pass, spending two weeks and a small fee instead of a painful year.

items.title: Hire people who owned hard problems early; give juniors real responsibility
triggers: how to spot good early hires
what to look for in a candidate

content.summary: Favor people who've owned hard problems end-to-end early in their lives or careers; give promising juniors real responsibility rather than busywork.
content.when_to_use: When assessing early hires where you can't afford big salaries and pedigree is a weak signal anyway.
how_to_run: Probe for moments they owned an ambiguous, hard problem to completion — a project, a side business, a team they carried — and what they did when it went wrong.
Weight demonstrated ownership and learning speed over title or brand.
When you hire a junior, hand them a real outcome they're accountable for, with clear scope and support.
Give responsibility in increasing chunks, watching whether they reach for more or wait to be told.
Cut losses quickly if someone consistently needs the problem solved for them.
content.preconditions: You can supervise and support a junior so 'real responsibility' isn't abandonment; the stakes of any single mistake are survivable while they learn.
content.failure_modes: Romanticizing 'hunger' and hiring underqualified people for roles that genuinely need experience. Confusing real ownership with self-promotion. Giving responsibility without support and calling it 'trust,' then blaming them. Over-indexing on a good origin story while ignoring whether they can do this job now. Survivorship bias in the celebrated scrappy-hire stories.
content.evidence_note: Plausible, widely shared founder lore rather than a measured finding; 'early ownership predicts startup performance' has weak controlled evidence and is prone to survivorship bias. Useful as a tiebreaker and culture lens, not a reliable predictor — low, stated with caution.
content.regional_notes: Signals of 'owning hard problems early' vary by background: running a small business out of necessity, supporting a family, or building without resources are strong ownership signals pedigree-screening misses. Don't mistake polish or access for ability across regions and class backgrounds.
content.examples: A founder choosing between a credentialed big-company candidate and a junior who, with no budget, single-handedly built and ran a small community project through setbacks. For an early generalist role they take the junior with a real, scoped outcome and weekly support — while remembering that for a deep-specialist role, raw ownership alone wouldn't be enough.

items.title: Default alive or default dead?

content.summary: At your current spend and growth rate, do you reach profitability before the cash runs out? If the honest answer is no, act now — cut costs or raise — while you still have options.
content.when_to_use: Whenever cash is a concern, before raising, and at every budget decision.
how_to_run: Project cash out month by month at current burn and growth
See if the line crosses profitable before it crosses zero
If default dead, change spend or growth now — not after another quarter
content.failure_modes: Avoiding the honest math until cash is nearly gone; assuming growth will accelerate to cover burn without evidence; treating a hoped-for raise as a plan rather than a possibility.
content.evidence_note: High — a clarifying, widely-used lens; the danger is founders who avoid doing the math until it's too late.

items.title: Always know your runway and the exact milestone the next money must buy
triggers: how much runway do I have
when do I run out of money
am I going to make it

content.summary: Always know how many months of runway you have left and the exact milestone the next money must buy.
content.when_to_use: Continuously, but especially before raising, before any big hire or spend, and at the start of each quarter.
how_to_run: Compute runway: cash on hand divided by net monthly burn — recompute whenever burn changes.
Name the single milestone the next money must buy (the proof that unlocks the round after, or reaching profitability).
Work backward from that milestone to the cash and time it actually requires, plus a buffer for slippage.
If raising, start while you still have ~12+ months left — raising takes months and desperation kills leverage.
Track both numbers on one visible page, updated monthly, so a cash surprise never happens.
content.preconditions: You can see your actual cash and burn (basic bookkeeping). If finances are opaque, fixing that visibility is step zero.
content.failure_modes: Discovering you're nearly out of cash with no time to raise or cut, forcing a down round or shutdown. Raising against a vague 'grow the business' instead of a concrete milestone. Under-buffering and assuming the milestone arrives on schedule. Starting a raise at 3 months left, removing all leverage. Counting committed-but-unpaid cash as if banked.
content.evidence_note: About as close to consensus as startup finance gets — know runway and tie raises to milestones, with common targets of 18-24+ months and starting to raise with 12+ months left (NYU, JPMorgan guidance, startups.com). High.
content.regional_notes: Runway targets shift with the funding climate — looser markets tolerate thinner runway, tighter ones demand more. Outside the largest hubs, rounds take longer to close, so add margin. Currency volatility can silently shorten runway for teams spending in a different currency than they hold.
content.examples: A founder has 9 months of cash and assumes they'll 'raise when they need to.' Since raising typically takes ~6 months, they'd be fundraising from near-desperation almost immediately. They instead define the milestone the next round must buy, cut a non-essential cost to stretch runway, and begin conversations early while they still have leverage.

items.title: Bootstrapping keeps control and forces discipline — unless speed is the game

content.summary: Refusing outside money keeps control and forces profitability — great in most markets, dangerous in a winner-take-all race where a funded rival can win first.
content.when_to_use: When deciding whether to raise at all. To pressure-test whether your market rewards staying lean and self-funded, or whether it's a race where capital decides the winner.
how_to_run: Ask what your growth logic actually requires — can you reach a real business on revenue, or does winning demand outspending rivals fast?
Judge the market structure: in winner-take-all markets with strong network effects or high acquisition costs, being out-capitalized is a structural disadvantage.
If the market rewards patience and margins, bootstrapping buys control, focus, and freedom from the raise treadmill.
If it's a land grab, be honest that a well-funded competitor moving first can foreclose the market regardless of how good you are.
Reassess as conditions change — bootstrapping now doesn't forbid raising later.
content.preconditions: You have a path to revenue (or savings) that can sustain the business while it grows; you genuinely understand whether your market is winner-take-all.
content.failure_modes: Bootstrapping on principle into a winner-take-all market and watching a funded rival capture it. Conversely, raising and burning fast in a market that never needed it, then dying when targets aren't met. Mistaking 'I survived bootstrapped' stories for proof it's safer (survivorship bias). Starving a genuinely capital-hungry opportunity into mediocrity.
content.evidence_note: The control/discipline upside and the winner-take-all caveat are both well-argued and widely held. Critically, the 'bootstrapped startups win more' claim is challenged as survivorship bias collapsing different company populations. So the principle is sound but path-dependent, not universally superior — medium.
content.regional_notes: Where venture capital is scarce, bootstrapping is often the only realistic option, making capital-efficient models valuable — but a foreign funded entrant can sometimes outspend a strong local team. Where grants/non-dilutive funding exist, they extend the bootstrapped path without giving up control.
content.examples: A founder building niche software for a specialized profession debates raising. The market is fragmented, sticky, and not winner-take-all, so staying bootstrapped keeps control and reaches profitability on their terms. A different founder building a consumer marketplace — where one network usually dominates — concludes the opposite: a funded competitor could lock up the market first.

items.title: Money amplifies a working machine; it can't fix a broken one
triggers: should I raise to fix growth
is now the right time to raise
money to grow vs money to survive
do I have product-market fit yet

content.summary: Money amplifies a machine that already works; it doesn't fix one that's broken.
content.when_to_use: When considering raising to solve a problem — slow growth, weak retention, unclear demand. To check whether you'd accelerate something proven or paper over something unproven.
how_to_run: Identify what specifically isn't working and ask: is this 'not enough fuel' or 'the engine doesn't run'?
Look for a repeatable, working loop (customers find you, get value, stay or pay) — capital scales loops, it doesn't create them.
If the loop works, raising to pour fuel on it is exactly right.
If the loop is broken, fix the machine first with the cash you have; raising now just burns faster and buys a louder failure.
Pressure-test with unit economics: would 10x spend produce ~10x result, or just 10x the loss?
content.preconditions: You can tell whether your core loop works (usage, retention, or revenue data). Pre-evidence, assume the machine is unproven.
content.failure_modes: Raising to 'fix' growth that's really a product/retention problem, then scaling the leak. Using fresh capital to mask absent demand, delaying the hard truth. Pouring fuel on a motion that only worked at tiny scale. Mistaking a temporary spike for a working machine. Over-hiring on the new cash before the loop is repeatable.
content.evidence_note: Near-universal operating wisdom — scaling before product-market fit is one of the most cited causes of startup failure, and 'capital amplifies, doesn't create' is broadly endorsed. Robust and consistent enough to grade high, even as a principle rather than a single statistic.
content.examples: A founder with flat usage believes a raise will fix it by funding marketing. But the few users who try it don't come back — a broken machine, not a fuel shortage. They hold off, fix retention with the cash they have, and only once people stick raise to pour fuel on a motion that now actually works.

items.title: Grow predictably, sustainably, profitably, de-risked — and keep about a year of cash
triggers: is growth at all costs smart

content.summary: Grow in a way that's predictable, sustainable, profitable, and de-risked — and keep roughly a year of cash in reserve.
content.when_to_use: When setting growth targets and deciding how aggressive to be, especially once you have real revenue. To resist 'growth at all costs' and set a cash-reserve floor.
how_to_run: Aim for growth you can predict and repeat, not one-off spikes you can't explain.
Prefer growth that improves (or doesn't destroy) unit economics — growing while bleeding more per customer is fragile.
Actively de-risk: reduce dependence on a single customer, channel, or person as you grow.
Hold a cash buffer of roughly a year so a shock or missed quarter isn't existential.
If hitting all four at once isn't possible, treat that tension as a signal to slow down rather than gamble the company.
content.preconditions: Enough revenue or capital that a ~1-year buffer is achievable; pre-revenue, the relevant principle is runway-and-milestone (know-your-number).
content.failure_modes: Chasing headline growth that's unpredictable and unprofitable, then collapsing when it can't sustain. Running the buffer too thin chasing speed. Over-conservatism — hoarding cash while a genuine winner-take-all window closes. Concentration risk hidden under good top-line numbers. Treating the buffer as untouchable even for a clearly correct, time-sensitive bet.
content.evidence_note: The discipline and a ~12-month buffer reflect mainstream prudent-growth and runway guidance (JPMorgan and CFO guides recommend 12-24+ months). Sensible and widely advised, but the four-part formulation and exact one-year figure are heuristics, not validated constants — medium.
content.regional_notes: In tighter funding climates or markets where raising is slow, a larger buffer is prudent. Seasonal/lumpy revenue should size the buffer to the worst trough. Currency exposure can erode a buffer that looks adequate on paper.
content.examples: A founder with growing revenue is pushed to spend the whole balance to triple next quarter. The spike wouldn't be repeatable, would worsen per-customer economics, and would drop reserves near zero. They instead set a year-of-cash floor, grow at a sustainable pace, and add a second sales channel so they're not betting everything on one.

items.title: 'Trust your gut and bet the company' is mostly luck retold as wisdom

content.summary: 'Trust your gut and bet the entire company' is mostly survivorship bias — the winners get retold as visionary, the identical losers are forgotten.
content.when_to_use: When you're being urged (by others or yourself) to make an irreversible, company-staking bet on conviction alone. Use it as a brake to force a check on whether this is judgment or a coin flip dressed up as destiny.
how_to_run: Notice the stakes: if losing this bet ends the company, it's a one-way door deserving maximum scrutiny.
Separate the advice from the outcome — 'bet it all and it worked' tells you nothing without the unseen founders who bet it all and lost.
Ask what evidence beyond gut supports the bet; if the honest answer is 'just conviction,' treat it as a gamble.
Look for a version that captures most of the upside without staking the whole company (a staged or reversible bet).
If you still proceed, do it eyes-open as a calculated gamble you can articulate.
content.failure_modes: Staking the company on a hunch and losing everything with no second move. Mistaking confidence for information. Copying a celebrated 'all-in' story whose survival was luck. Ignoring reversible alternatives. Advisors who profit from your swing encouraging it. Over-applying the warning to talk yourself out of genuinely sound asymmetric bets — the point is to demand evidence, not to never bet.
content.evidence_note: Graded low and flagged. The 'trust your gut, bet the company' genre is a textbook survivorship-bias artifact: identical bold bets that failed are unremembered, so surviving stories overstate the gut's wisdom. No sound evidence company-staking conviction bets beat chance.
content.examples: A founder is urged to pour every remaining dollar into one unproven pivot because 'the greats all trusted their gut.' Stepping back, that story only survives for the ones who happened to be right. They fund a small reversible test of the pivot first, treating the all-or-nothing version as the gamble it is, not as wisdom.

items.title: Do things that don't scale (early)

content.summary: Hand-recruit and over-serve your first users — personally onboard them, do the unscalable things. Scalable channels come AFTER something people love exists.
content.failure_modes: Reaching for ads/automation before you have love and retention; treating early users as a funnel instead of relationships.

items.title: Get one acquisition channel working first

content.summary: Get a single acquisition channel working to a real, repeatable number before adding a second. Most startups die with zero channels mastered, not too few tried.
how_to_run: Pick one channel that fits where your buyers already are
Push it until it reliably produces customers at sane cost
Only then add a second channel
content.failure_modes: Dabbling in five channels so none gets enough effort to prove out; declaring a channel dead before a fair test; scaling spend on a channel before the unit economics actually work.

items.title: No plan to reach buyers
triggers: build it and they will come
relying on word of mouth only

content.summary: A great product with no concrete plan to reach buyers loses to a worse product that's easy to find.
content.when_to_use: When you're about to build or launch and your plan for getting customers is vague ('we'll do some marketing,' 'it'll spread on its own'), or when a finished product gets little traction and you can't name how your users arrived.
how_to_run: Before building further, write the one or two specific channels where you'll reach the first 100 users, and why each is realistic for you.
For your top channel, estimate the rough cost or effort to acquire one customer end-to-end (your time included) vs what a customer is worth.
Run a small, cheap test on that channel now, before the product is polished, and measure whether strangers respond.
Treat distribution as a feature with its own build time: equal weight to the product, revisit monthly.
content.preconditions: You have a clear idea of who the buyer is; a channel test is meaningless if you can't name the person you're reaching.
content.failure_modes: Assuming the product will spread by itself and discovering post-launch that no channel was built. Counting friends and your network as proof of a channel that won't scale to strangers. Picking a channel popular in articles but where your buyers don't gather. Ignoring the full cost to acquire (your unpaid hours, tools). Spreading thin across five channels so none gets enough effort.
content.evidence_note: Widely reported in startup post-mortems that poor distribution outranks bad product as a failure cause, and that founders underestimate true acquisition cost by omitting their own time. High on direction, though exact percentages vary and are soft.
content.regional_notes: Viable channels differ sharply by region: the dominant search engine, app store, messaging app, or social platform your buyers use may not be what you assume. Some markets run distribution through a few gatekeeper platforms or super-apps.
content.examples: A solo technical founder spends nine months building a polished scheduling tool, planning to 'post it online and let it grow.' At launch it gets 30 signups, all friends. Forced to name a real channel, she realizes her buyers (clinic managers) live in two niche professional forums; a week of helpful posting there brings more qualified signups than the whole launch.

items.title: Grow from one clean product

content.summary: Build one clean product, grow from profits, say no to one-off custom requests.
content.when_to_use: When deciding whether to raise outside money or grow on your own revenue, or when a paying customer asks for custom work that would pull the product in a new direction.
how_to_run: Keep the product narrow: define the single core thing it does and reinvest early profits into making that better rather than adding adjacent features.
Set a rule for custom requests: take them only if many customers want the same thing (then it's a product feature) and decline true one-offs even when the money tempts.
Run lean enough that revenue covers costs, so growth pace is set by what customers pay, not a runway clock.
Revisit periodically whether you've hit a ceiling that genuinely requires outside capital, and raise deliberately if so.
content.preconditions: You have, or can quickly reach, paying customers; organic growth needs revenue to reinvest, so it doesn't fit a long pre-revenue research phase.
content.failure_modes: Saying yes to lucrative custom work and slowly becoming a consultancy disguised as a product company. Confusing 'no outside money' with 'no ambition' and under-investing while a funded competitor outruns you in a winner-take-most market. Starving the business of needed hires to stay 'lean,' causing burnout or quality collapse. Treating bootstrapping as identity rather than strategy.
content.evidence_note: Self-funded firms show higher reported survival and earlier profitability in several datasets, but the comparison is heavily self-selecting and survival figures vary wildly between sources. Medium: sound for many businesses but not a universal win, and headline statistics are unreliable.
