content.regional_notes: In regions with thin venture markets, organic growth is often the only realistic path. Where capital is abundant and markets reward speed, pure bootstrapping can be a disadvantage in land-grab categories.
content.examples: A two-person team launches a focused invoicing app. A large client offers a sizeable fee for a bespoke reporting module only they'd use. They decline the one-off and instead ship a smaller reporting feature several customers requested, funded by existing subscriptions; a year later the business profitably supports both founders with no outside money.

items.title: Win one market, then expand
triggers: save the hard market for last

content.summary: Don't launch everywhere at once; win one market, learn, expand, save the hardest for last.
content.when_to_use: When choosing where to launch among several possible markets, segments, or regions, or when feeling pressure to expand to a second market before the first is clearly working.
how_to_run: Pick one narrow market where you can plausibly become the obvious best choice, favoring one you can reach and serve well over the biggest.
Define 'won' concretely (repeatable sales, healthy margins, manageable support) and only expand once the first market hits it.
Order the remaining markets easiest to hardest and expand in sequence, carrying forward what you learned.
Deliberately save the largest or most defended market for last, when you're strongest.
content.preconditions: The markets must be different enough that winning one teaches something transferable; if identical, sequencing buys less.
content.failure_modes: Declaring the first market 'won' on vanity signs (signups, press) rather than repeatable paid sales, then expanding onto a weak base. Opening a second front to chase a competitor before the first pays. Sequencing so slowly a faster rival locks up adjacent markets. Choosing a glamorous hard market first and burning resources before learning the cheap lessons.
content.evidence_note: Strongly supported: premature scaling (including into too many markets at once before product-market fit) is repeatedly identified as a top failure pattern (Startup Genome), and staged entry with payback thresholds is standard. High.
content.regional_notes: Regional expansion adds language, payments, legal, and trust barriers that compound if taken in parallel; international returns can take years to mature, which is why stage-gating by evidence matters. The 'easiest first' market is often home or a culturally adjacent one.
content.examples: A founder with a delivery-logistics app is tempted to launch in three cities at once. Instead she wins one mid-size city first, where she can personally fix problems, until orders repeat and unit economics turn positive. The hardened playbook and software let her open the next two cities far more cheaply, leaving the hyper-competitive capital city for last.

items.title: Stack services on a daily habit

content.summary: Once you own a daily habit you can stack services — only after the first truly works and pays.
content.when_to_use: When you already own a frequent, sticky user habit and are considering adding adjacent services, or when tempted to bundle many offerings before the core is genuinely loved and profitable.
how_to_run: Confirm the core is a real daily-or-near-daily habit with strong retention and that it pays for itself before adding anything.
Add one adjacent service the same users would naturally want in the same moment, and check it deepens engagement rather than clutters.
Keep the core experience clean: if a new service degrades the thing people show up for, it's not worth the bundle.
Expand the stack one service at a time, each earning its place on usage and economics.
content.preconditions: You genuinely own the habit first: high retention and frequent use. Bundling onto a product people don't return to multiplies weakness.
content.failure_modes: Bundling to mask a core that hasn't achieved retention or profitability. Adding services users don't associate with your app, so each needs its own expensive acquisition. Cluttering the core until the original habit weakens. Assuming the dominant-platform bundling you've seen elsewhere will work without the underlying daily lock-in those platforms had first.
content.evidence_note: Observable in dominant consumer platforms, but survivorship-heavy: the visible successes had enormous, genuinely sticky cores first, and countless attempts to bundle without that core failed quietly. Medium and flagged: the sequence (habit first, then stack) matters far more than the bundling idea itself.
content.regional_notes: Multi-service bundling is most established where a few apps became default daily utilities and payments, messaging, and commerce converged; much harder where users keep separate single-purpose apps and switching is easy.
content.examples: A team builds a messaging tool a niche community opens many times a day with strong retention. Only after that habit is undeniable and covering costs do they add a simple payments feature for the exact moment users coordinate money in chat. It lifts engagement. They resist adding a marketplace and a booking tool simultaneously, adding one more service only once payments proves itself.

items.title: Reversibility — one-way vs two-way door

content.summary: Can you undo it cheaply? Two-way door (reversible): decide fast, the cost of finding out is tiny. One-way door (irreversible): slow down and think hard. The classic trap is agonizing over a reversible thing while rushing an irreversible one.
content.when_to_use: On almost any decision the founder is stuck on — it sets the tempo.
how_to_run: Ask: if this is wrong, how cheaply can I reverse it?
Reversible → move now, treat it as a test
Irreversible → run a quick premortem first
Name the trap if they're over-thinking a cheap test or rushing a permanent choice
content.failure_modes: Agonizing over a cheap reversible test while rushing a one-way door; treating a genuinely irreversible move as reversible because deciding fast feels good; using buckets to avoid thinking rather than to speed it up.
content.evidence_note: High — a robust, widely-used decision lens; the value is in correctly classifying the door.

items.title: Premortem — imagine it already failed

content.summary: Before an irreversible commitment, imagine it's a year later and it failed. Write the top 3 reasons. De-risk those cheaply before you commit.
content.when_to_use: Before any one-way door: a co-founder, a big hire, a raise, a public pivot.
content.failure_modes: Running it as a box-ticking ritual and ignoring the risks it surfaces; naming only comfortable risks; using it to justify endless delay instead of de-risking the top few and moving.

items.title: The cheapest decisive test

content.summary: When stuck, find the smallest experiment that actually resolves the uncertainty — and run it this week, not someday. The goal is to learn the one thing you don't know, cheaply.
how_to_run: Name the single biggest unknown
Design the smallest test that would change your decision
Run it this week; pre-commit to what each result means
content.failure_modes: Designing a test so small or vague it cannot actually change the decision; running it someday instead of this week; ignoring the result because it was inconvenient.

items.title: Decide the stop rule first
triggers: how long do we try this

content.summary: Before a test or bet, write what result means keep-going vs stop, then honor it.
content.when_to_use: Before running any meaningful experiment, launch, or time-boxed bet, especially one you'll be emotionally invested in, so the decision rule is set while you can think clearly.
how_to_run: Before starting, write the specific result that means 'keep going' and the one that means 'stop,' with a number and a deadline where possible.
Decide in advance what you'll do in each case, so the test has real consequences.
Run the test without changing the threshold midway; note the result against the line you drew.
If the result says stop, honor it, or explicitly and openly revise the rule with a stated reason, never silently.
content.preconditions: The bet must have an observable outcome within a defined window; open-ended efforts with no measurable signal can't be pre-committed this way.
content.failure_modes: Moving the goalposts after seeing the result because you're attached ('it almost worked, one more month'). Setting a threshold so vague any outcome reads as success. A deadline with no teeth, so 'stop' never triggers. Writing kill criteria but burying them. Honoring the rule so rigidly you ignore a genuinely changed situation rather than openly revising it.
content.evidence_note: Pre-committing to decision criteria is a well-established debiasing practice against sunk-cost and confirmation bias, and time-boxed kill/continue gates are standard in disciplined experimentation. High: the mechanism is robust, even though the hard part (actually honoring it) is behavioral.
content.examples: A founder runs a paid-ads test and writes beforehand: 'If cost to get one signup is under X within three weeks, I keep building; if above 2X, I stop and rethink.' At week three it lands well above 2X. Tempted to blame the ad copy and extend, he honors the rule, pauses building, and goes back to talking to users — saving months he'd have spent forcing a weak idea.

items.title: Cut fast, pay your own way

content.summary: When money gets tight, stop chasing growth; cut fast, aim to pay your own way.
content.when_to_use: When runway is shrinking and fresh funding is uncertain, or when growth is being bought with money you no longer have, and survival now matters more than expansion.
how_to_run: Calculate exactly how many months of cash you have left at current burn, and treat that as the clock governing every decision.
Cut deep and once rather than shallow and repeatedly: remove spending that buys growth you can't afford, aiming to reach revenue-covers-costs.
Refocus the team on the customers and product that already pay, dropping far-future bets.
Set a clear break-even target and date; reaching it converts the company from dependent on outside money to able to choose its next move.
content.preconditions: There must be some paying revenue or a near-term path to it; if the business has no way to charge, cutting alone reaches break-even only by reaching zero.
content.failure_modes: Cutting in slow, shallow rounds that demoralize the team and never reach break-even. Cutting the very things that generate revenue (sales, core product) along with the waste. Waiting too long out of optimism, so cuts happen from weakness with no runway to execute. Treating the pivot as permanent retreat and killing all growth ambition.
content.evidence_note: Cutting burn to reach default-alive / break-even when capital tightens is standard, widely-endorsed crisis guidance (reinforced every funding downturn) and follows directly from the runway arithmetic. High.
content.regional_notes: In regions with shallow capital markets, reaching break-even is often the only safety net, so the instinct to fund growth from revenue should kick in earlier. Local labor laws can make cuts slower and costlier, arguing for acting sooner.
content.examples: A startup burning heavily on ads has eight months of cash and no committed next round. The founders cut the paid-growth spend and a speculative second product, refocus on the segment already paying, and reset everyone around a break-even target six months out. Growth flattens but the company stops needing investors to survive, and later raises on its own terms.

items.title: Sell to someone who can win

content.summary: Selling to a bigger player who can win the war can beat losing it slowly alone.
content.when_to_use: When you're in a fight you're likely to lose on your own (out-resourced, out-distributed) and a credible buyer could carry the mission, team, or product further than you can — especially while you still have leverage to negotiate.
how_to_run: Assess honestly whether you can realistically win independently, separating 'hard but winnable' from 'slowly losing.'
Identify buyers for whom your team, product, or position is genuinely valuable, and engage while your metrics still give leverage.
Weigh the concrete outcome for your team and yourself (roles, payout, mission continuing) against the realistic outcome of fighting on alone.
Decide deliberately and, once you commit, treat it as one-way: selling closes the independent path.
content.preconditions: There must be a buyer who actually wants what you have; an exit is only an option if someone credible values the team, product, or position.
content.failure_modes: Waiting until nearly out of cash, so you negotiate from desperation and it becomes a fire-sale. Refusing a fair soft landing out of pride and running into an uncontrolled shutdown. Overvaluing the company on past hopes and rejecting reasonable offers that won't return. Selling too early out of fear when the fight was winnable. Ignoring the human terms (roles, earn-outs) and accepting a deal the team resents.
content.evidence_note: Acqui-hires and strategic sales are documented as common, often-responsible 'soft landings' that beat uncontrolled shutdowns for teams, though founders/investors often recoup less than hoped and the press-release 'acquisition' overstates the economics. Medium: clearly better than a hard failure in many cases, but outcomes vary widely and it's irreversible.
content.regional_notes: Acquirer appetite varies by region and cycle; in talent-scarce or AI-driven markets acqui-hire terms can be stronger, while in thin M&A markets a credible buyer may simply not exist.
content.examples: A founder building a niche developer tool sees two well-funded competitors out-distributing her despite a better product; on the current path she's slowly losing. A larger platform offers to acquire the product and team while her numbers still look healthy. Rather than grind to a shutdown, she negotiates from that position: the team gets good roles, the product lives on, and she avoids two more years losing the war alone.

items.title: Spreading thin isn't winning
triggers: growth looks good but no profit

content.summary: Spreading everywhere looks like winning until none of it pays; prove one market first.
content.when_to_use: When operating across many markets, segments, or product lines at once and growth metrics look impressive but profitability and proof of a working model in any single one are missing.
how_to_run: List every market, segment, or line you're spread across and mark which actually pay for themselves.
Pick the single one with the best chance of a working, profitable model and concentrate resources there until it genuinely works.
Pause or cut the fronts that only consume cash and attention without proof, treating broad presence as a cost, not an achievement.
Only re-expand from the proven base, one front at a time, using what you learned.
content.preconditions: At least one of your current fronts must be capable of becoming profitable; if none can, the problem is the model itself, not the spread.
content.failure_modes: Mistaking activity and top-line growth across many fronts for progress while every front loses money. Refusing to cut weak markets because retreating 'looks like losing,' and bleeding out across all. Concentrating on the wrong front (the biggest, not the most provable). Cutting so indiscriminately you kill the one front about to work.
content.evidence_note: Strongly supported: scaling across too many markets or lines before proving a model is a core premature-scaling failure pattern repeatedly tied to startup death (Startup Genome). High and flagged because the trap disguises itself as success.
content.regional_notes: Multi-region spread compounds the danger: each market adds fixed overhead (legal, payments, support, localization) that makes 'a little everywhere' especially cash-hungry and hard to unwind.
content.examples: A founder expands into five cities and three segments within a year; the dashboard shows rising users and it feels like winning. But no single city or segment is profitable and cash is draining. Forced to mark what actually pays, only one segment in one city breaks even. They pull back to it, get it genuinely profitable, then re-expand deliberately, one front at a time.

items.title: First principles — reason up from what's actually true
triggers: why do we do it this way

content.summary: Strip a problem down to the things you know are actually true, then build your answer back up from those, instead of copying what everyone else does. It helps when 'the way it's done' makes something seem impossible or expensive when it might not be.
content.when_to_use: When you're about to copy a competitor or follow 'industry standard' without knowing why; when something looks too expensive or impossible and you suspect the assumption, not the physics, is the blocker.
how_to_run: Write the decision as a goal, not the usual solution
List every assumption and mark each: proven, probably true, or just inherited
Throw out the inherited ones; keep what you can defend with facts or a real constraint
Rebuild the cheapest path that satisfies what survived
Check: does the rebuilt plan actually beat the default?
content.preconditions: You have enough domain knowledge to tell a real constraint from a convention; if you can't tell, gather facts first.
content.failure_modes: Slow and overkill for routine calls. Beginners mislabel a real constraint (regulation, physics, hard cost) as a 'mere assumption' and build a plan that can't work. Breeds arrogance ('everyone else is dumb') — conventions often encode hard-won lessons you can't see yet.
content.evidence_note: Traces to Aristotle; standard in physics/engineering. As a named business tool it's popularized aphorism, not a tested method — no studies show it produces better startup decisions. A useful discipline for questioning assumptions, not proof your conclusion is right.
content.examples: A founder assumes she needs a $30k custom app to test a tutoring marketplace because 'all marketplaces have apps.' From first principles the real requirement is: connect a student to a tutor and take a cut — doable first with a web form, a shared calendar, and manual matching for the first 20 bookings, proving demand for a few hundred dollars.

items.title: Inversion — work backwards from what would make this fail
triggers: how do i not screw this up

content.summary: Instead of only asking 'how do I succeed?', ask 'what would guarantee this fails?' and make sure you don't do those things. It's easier to spot and dodge obvious ways to lose than to engineer a win.
content.when_to_use: When you're excited and want a reality check; before committing real money, time, or reputation; when you keep listing reasons it'll work but haven't asked how it dies.
how_to_run: Flip it: 'It's a year from now and this failed badly. What happened?'
Brainstorm every plausible cause of that failure
Sort by likely and fatal
For the top few, decide the cheap step that removes the risk now
Build those guardrails in before proceeding
content.failure_modes: Overused it becomes paralysis — cap it to risks both likely and fatal. Biases toward inaction, itself a risk for a startup that must learn fast. Listing risks is not managing them — founders feel safer just naming dangers without changing anything.
content.evidence_note: Ancient (Stoic premeditation); popularized via Jacobi's 'invert, always invert' and Munger. Strongest empirical cousin is the pre-mortem (Gary Klein): prospective hindsight improves identifying failure reasons. Mechanism has support; the broad life-advice version is folklore.
content.examples: A founder is about to spend three months building before launch. Inversion asks 'why did this flop?' — top honest answer: 'I built for months and nobody wanted it.' That reorders the plan: week one is 15 user conversations and a fake sign-up page, so he learns whether anyone wants it before the three months are spent.

items.title: Second-order thinking — ask 'and then what?'

content.summary: Don't stop at the first, obvious result of a choice — keep asking 'and then what happens?' for two or three more steps. It catches cases where an action that looks great now sets off later effects that undo it.
content.when_to_use: When an option looks obviously good and easy — exactly when second-order effects get missed; for pricing, discounts, hiring, fundraising, growth hacks; when a quick fix is tempting.
how_to_run: Write the immediate effect everyone sees
Ask 'and then what?' — how customers, competitors, and your team react
Ask again one or two more times
Check whether a later effect cancels the benefit you wanted
Decide with the whole chain in view
content.failure_modes: You can't forecast far-out effects reliably — past two or three steps it's confident guessing. Can become an excuse for inaction. Tempts over-cleverness: assuming the 'obvious' move is a trap when sometimes it's just correct.
content.evidence_note: Popularized as 'second-level thinking' by investor Howard Marks (2011); overlaps with systems thinking and unintended consequences, which are well established. No controlled evidence it improves decisions on demand — a sound habit with a strong conceptual basis, not a validated technique.
content.examples: A founder considers a steep launch discount. First order: sign-ups jump. And then what? Those users churn when it ends, paid conversion looks terrible, and word spreads that the 'real' price is the discount. She instead offers a small founding-member perk that doesn't anchor the price low.

items.title: Opportunity cost — what you give up by choosing this
triggers: should i be doing this instead

content.summary: The real cost of any choice isn't its price — it's the best other thing you could have done with that time or money. Founders have almost no spare time or cash, so every 'yes' is silently a 'no' to something that might matter more.
content.when_to_use: Deciding what to work on this week, which feature to build, whether to take a meeting or side project, tool vs hire; whenever you feel busy but unsure it's the right busy.
how_to_run: Name what this choice consumes — hours, cash, focus
Ask: with that same time/money, what's the single best alternative?
Compare against that alternative, not against doing nothing
If the alternative clearly moves your top goal more, do that
Make the trade explicit so you choose it on purpose
content.failure_modes: Can spiral into paralysis if you weigh every option against every other — reserve for choices consuming meaningful time/money. Grass-is-greener: you'll overrate what you didn't pick. Misused to frame rest or necessary slow work as 'wasted,' which burns founders out.
content.evidence_note: Foundational, non-controversial in economics. 'Opportunity-cost neglect' is documented (Frederick et al. 2009; a 2023 meta-analysis of 39 studies, N≈14,005, confirms the effect is robust though smaller than first claimed). Both concept and the tendency to ignore it are well-supported.
content.examples: A technical founder spends two weeks building a slick admin dashboard because coding is satisfying. The opportunity cost is two weeks not spent talking to customers — the thing that decides whether the product is worth building. The dashboard waits; he uses a spreadsheet and spends the two weeks on conversations.

items.title: Regret minimization — decide from your older self's view

content.summary: Picture yourself much older looking back, and choose the option you'd be least likely to regret. Useful for big, scary forks where fear of failing right now drowns out what you'll actually care about over a whole life.
content.when_to_use: Rare, high-stakes life forks — quitting a stable job, moving countries, taking or passing a big bet — where short-term fear is loud and fear of looking foolish is the main blocker.
how_to_run: Frame the two paths: do it vs don't
Imagine yourself at 80 — which path would you more likely regret, including never having tried?
Separate regret of trying-and-failing (often brief) from never-knowing (often lasting)
Reality-check: can you actually afford the downside?
Choose the lower lasting regret you can also survive
content.preconditions: Best for genuinely big, infrequent, hard-to-reverse choices; for everyday reversible decisions it's overkill.
content.failure_modes: Quietly biases toward bold action — ruinous if you can't afford the downside (betting money you can't lose). Ignores probabilities and costs, so pair it with the real odds and what failure costs. Classic survivorship trap: the 'I went for it' stories survive; the people who went broke don't get retold.
content.evidence_note: The 'imagine yourself at 80' version is an aphorism tied to Bezos's 1994 Amazon decision — inspiring, but one anecdote. Real academic work exists: regret theory (Loomes & Sugden, Bell, Fishburn, 1982) models how anticipated regret shapes choices. 'People factor in regret' is supported; 'minimizing regret yields the best decision' is folklore.
content.regional_notes: The 'follow your gut, no regrets, just leap' framing is loud in US startup lore and can read as reckless where families pool finances or a failed venture carries lasting stigma. The honest weighing of survivable-downside matters more where the safety net is thinner.
content.examples: A founder with six months of savings is paralyzed about leaving a stable job. The lens: at 80, would she more regret a six-month funded attempt that might fail, or never trying? Never-trying is heavier — but the lens also forces the reality check: she caps the experiment at six months with a hard 'go back to a job' line, so the leap is bold and survivable.

items.title: Think in odds, not certainties

content.summary: Instead of 'will this work or not?', ask 'how likely is this to work?' and put a rough number on it. The world runs on odds, not guarantees, so thinking in percentages keeps you honest about what you actually know.
content.when_to_use: When you catch yourself in absolutes ('this will definitely work' / 'this is hopeless'); when a decision hinges on an uncertain event; when fear or excitement pushes a yes/no story.
how_to_run: Write the claim you're treating as certain
Replace it with a percentage and say why that number
List 2-3 other ways it could go with rough odds (sum to ~100%)
Ask what info would move your number, and whether you can cheaply get it first
content.preconditions: The outcome is genuinely uncertain and depends on factors you can't fully control. For simple look-up facts, just find the answer.
content.failure_modes: The number can feel like knowledge when it's a guess dressed up. Founders overrate their own plan's odds. Tempts treating a 20%-chance catastrophe as 'unlikely, ignore it' when the downside is fatal. Use rough bands, state reasoning, never let a tidy number replace asking how bad the bad case is.
content.evidence_note: Foundational to statistics and decision theory. That assigning probabilities improves real-world judgment is supported by forecasting research, notably Tetlock's superforecasters who think in fine-grained odds and beat experts speaking in certainties. Strong for the practice; the hard part is human estimates are often miscalibrated.
content.examples: Deciding whether to quit your job next month: instead of 'I think it'll work,' you write '~60% I'll have one paying customer by then, ~25% enough revenue to cover rent within six months.' Seeing 25% on paper, you line up three months of savings and keep the job until a clear revenue signal.

items.title: Weigh the payoff by the odds

content.summary: A choice isn't just how big the prize is or how likely you are to win — it's both multiplied together. A small chance at a huge payoff can beat a sure thing, and a likely small win can beat a tempting long shot.
content.when_to_use: Weighing a risky move against a safer one, choosing between bets you can't all make, or tempted by a long shot. Best when you can roughly estimate both size and likelihood of outcomes.
how_to_run: List possible outcomes (best, likely, worst)
Estimate a rough payoff for each, including negatives
Put rough probabilities on each (sum ~100%)
Multiply payoff by probability, add up, compare to other options
If a 'bad case' could end the company, treat it as a veto, not a number in the average
content.preconditions: You can estimate rough payoffs and odds and will face many decisions over time so averages matter. For a single bet-the-company choice, EV informs but shouldn't decide alone.
content.failure_modes: The math hides ruin: great-EV bets can still wipe you out if the bad case is bankruptcy, because you play once and can't average across tries. Garbage inputs give confident garbage. Founders overweight the jackpot and underweight the boring likely case. Always look at the worst case on its own first.
content.evidence_note: Core proven result of probability theory (Pascal, Huygens, 17th c.); backbone of decision theory, insurance, finance. The honest caveat, since Bernoulli's 1738 St. Petersburg paradox, is that raw EV ignores risk of ruin and how much a gain/loss actually matters to you — a tool for thinking, not an autopilot.
content.examples: A client offers $5,000 now or a revenue-share you estimate at 30% chance of $40k, 40% of $6k, 30% of $0 — averaging ~$14,400, far above $5,000. But you're three months from running out of cash, so the 30% of $0 could sink you. You take the $5,000; with six months' runway the revenue-share would be smarter.

items.title: Update your beliefs as evidence arrives

content.summary: Start with your best current guess, then nudge it up or down each time real evidence shows up — don't throw out everything you knew, but don't cling to your first belief either. How much you move depends on how strong and surprising the evidence is.
content.when_to_use: When early signals come in (first users, sales calls, a beta) and you're deciding whether they mean anything; when tempted to ignore inconvenient data or overreact to one result; when deciding whether to change course.
how_to_run: Write your starting belief and confidence ('70% sure people will pay')
When evidence arrives, ask: how likely was this if my belief were true vs false?
Nudge confidence toward what the evidence favors — a lot if strong/surprising, a little if weak/noisy
Re-decide on the updated number; keep the loop open
content.preconditions: You have a prior you can state and evidence arriving over time. Best when you log starting confidence before the data, so you can't fool yourself about what you 'always knew.'
content.failure_modes: People update too little (anchoring, ignoring contradicting data) or too much (one loud customer = the whole truth). A single data point is weak — small samples lie. Confirmation bias counts hits, forgets misses. Only as good as your prior. Write the prior down; weigh evidence by how much it could happen by chance.
content.evidence_note: Bayes' theorem (Thomas Bayes, 1763) is proven math underpinning modern statistics, spam filters, diagnosis. As literal in-head arithmetic it's impractical; as a discipline (state prior, move proportionally to evidence) it's strongly supported by forecasting research. Proven math; the folklore is pretending you can do exact arithmetic in your head.
content.examples: You're 70% sure small shops will pay for your inventory tool. The first 10 cold calls all say 'too expensive' — evidence, but 10 calls is a small, maybe unlucky sample, so you drop to ~50% rather than quitting. After 40 more with the same objection, you move to 25% and decide the issue is price — so you test a cheaper tier before concluding it's dead.

items.title: Build in buffer for being wrong

content.summary: Assume your estimates are too optimistic, because they usually are, and leave yourself a cushion so a normal miss doesn't kill you. The bigger the consequence of being wrong, the bigger the buffer.
content.when_to_use: Committing real resources on an estimate that could be off — budgets, runway, timelines, hiring, capacity. Especially when being wrong is expensive or irreversible: spending your last savings, signing a long lease, promising a launch date.
how_to_run: Make your honest best estimate of what you need
Assume it's optimistic — how has this estimate been wrong before?
Add a buffer sized to how bad the miss would be
Decide using the buffered number, not the rosy one — if it doesn't work with the buffer, the plan is too fragile
content.preconditions: Your decision depends on estimates that could be wrong and a miss has real cost. If a mistake is cheap and reversible, run leaner and skip the heavy buffer.
content.failure_modes: Too little: planning to exactly your best-case runway, so any normal slip becomes a crisis. Too much: hoarding cushion so you never ship, or padding so heavily the plan is uncompetitive. A buffer breeds false comfort — it protects against ordinary misses, not a broken plan or a black swan.
content.evidence_note: Popularized in investing by Benjamin Graham ('The Intelligent Investor', 1949); the underlying idea — engineers building bridges to hold several times rated load — is centuries old and physically proven. As a planning principle for uncertain estimates it's sound and widely endorsed; the caveat is no buffer protects against being wrong about the whole thesis.
content.examples: Your spreadsheet says break-even in 8 months, fitting your 9 months of savings. Knowing first-time timelines slip, you assume 12-14 months. That doesn't fit, so instead of quitting now you keep part-time income for six months and cut spending — buying enough buffer that a normal delay is a tight quarter, not shutting down.

items.title: Know the edge of what you understand

content.summary: It's fine to know a lot about a few things and little about the rest — what matters is being honest about where the line is. Make confident calls inside what you truly understand; slow down, learn, or get help outside it.
content.when_to_use: When a decision pulls you into unfamiliar territory (a technical founder facing pricing/sales, or a business founder facing architecture); when tempted to wing a high-stakes call; deciding whether to do it yourself, learn it, or bring in help.
how_to_run: Ask honestly: is this inside what I understand, near the edge, or outside?
Inside → decide and move. Near the edge → quick check with someone who knows.
Outside → don't fake it; invest time to learn it or get a trusted expert and pressure-test their advice
Notice 'how hard can it be?' — that feeling is usually the edge, not the center
content.preconditions: The decision needs specific knowledge and getting it wrong has real cost. Mostly irrelevant for low-stakes reversible choices where trying is cheaper than studying.
content.failure_modes: Overconfidence: smart people, especially technical founders, assume competence transfers ('I can code, so marketing is easy'). Opposite failure: using the circle as an excuse never to stretch — founders must operate outside comfort constantly. The boundary is self-assessed, easy to draw where it flatters you.
content.evidence_note: Articulated by Buffett and Munger as an investing discipline — influential folk wisdom, not a formally tested theory (hence medium). Rests on a real foundation: research on overconfidence and Dunning-Kruger shows low-skill people overestimate their skill. The bias is proven; the 'circle' framing is a useful heuristic, not a measured law.
content.examples: A strong engineer who built the product alone must set pricing. The instinct is to pick a number and move on, but that's the 'how hard can it be?' feeling — pricing is outside the circle. Instead of guessing on something that determines revenue, he spends two days reading how comparable tools price, runs his tiers past two founders who've sold to his market, and adjusts before launch.

items.title: Decide fast, then update

content.summary: Look at what's actually happening, make sense of it, pick a move, do it, then look again. The point is to go around this loop faster than the world changes on you.
content.when_to_use: When frozen by analysis, when the situation keeps shifting (a competitor moved, a channel died, demand spiked), or when you've been planning longer than doing. Best when feedback is cheap and fast.
how_to_run: Observe: write what you actually see now (real numbers, real customer words)
Orient: what does it mean, what changed, what's your biggest wrong assumption?
Decide: pick the smallest next move that gets new information
Act: do it this week, on a deadline
Loop: schedule the next observe before you start
content.preconditions: You can get feedback quickly and cheaply. If a decision is slow and expensive to reverse, slow down instead.
content.failure_modes: Confusing motion with progress: looping fast on tiny changes while ignoring a fundamental flaw. Skipping 'Orient' and just reacting. Using 'move fast' to dodge hard thinking. Vague enough to retrofit onto any decision, so it can feel insightful without changing what you do.
content.evidence_note: From USAF Colonel John Boyd (1970s-90s). Influential military/business folklore, not controlled-tested; historians note the core evidence was thin and the model loosely defined. A useful habit for fast iteration, not proof that speed always wins.
content.examples: You ran a paid-ad landing page: 200 clicks, zero signups. Instead of a two-week redesign, you observe (headline gets clicks, people leave at the price line), orient (price objection, not interest), decide (test a free-trial version), and act this week with one changed page — learning more in five days than a month of planning.

items.title: Is this a good market to be in

content.summary: Some industries are structurally hard to make money in, no matter how good you are. This checks five pressures (rivals, new entrants, substitutes, supplier power, buyer power) to see if profit is even possible here.
content.when_to_use: When choosing which market to enter, understanding why margins are thin, or noticing everyone competes on price. Useful once you have a real market in mind, not at the blank-idea stage.
how_to_run: Rivalry: how many real competitors, and do they fight on price?
New entrants: how easily can someone copy you next month?
Substitutes: what else solves the problem (including doing nothing or a spreadsheet)?
Buyer power: can a few big customers dictate your price?
Supplier power: can key suppliers or the one platform you depend on squeeze you?
content.preconditions: You have a specific market defined. It describes industry structure, not your individual edge — pair it with a view of your advantage.
content.failure_modes: Doing this at the idea stage instead of talking to customers — a beautiful five-page analysis of a market nobody wants. Assumes stable industries; underweights fast-moving tech and the chance a startup changes the structure. Treating a 'bad' industry as a stop sign when your specific wedge might still work.
content.evidence_note: Michael Porter (Harvard, 1979-80). One of the most studied, durable strategy frameworks, strong as a descriptive tool. Caveat: explains industry profit differences well but is weaker at predicting individual startup outcomes and was built for established industries, not pre-product founders.
content.examples: Choosing between a generic dropshipping store and a niche B2B compliance tool: Five Forces shows dropshipping has near-zero entry barriers, powerful suppliers, and price-shopping buyers (brutal margins forever), while the compliance tool has high switching costs and few substitutes once embedded. Same effort, structurally different odds — pick the harder-to-copy market.

items.title: What makes you hard to copy

content.summary: A real advantage isn't just being good today; it's having something competitors can't easily copy even when they want to: cost from scale, network effects, lock-in, brand, owning something scarce, a smarter process, or a move the incumbent won't dare match.
content.when_to_use: Working out why a customer would stay instead of switching, or planning how today's lead becomes durable. Most useful once you have real customers, not at first idea.
how_to_run: List who could copy you (a clone, an incumbent, the platform you sit on)
For each power type, ask honestly: do you have it, or just hope to?
Pick the one or two realistic for your business and stage
Name the specific barrier: what would a competitor have to spend or sacrifice to match you?
Choose actions that deepen that barrier
content.preconditions: You have a working product and some traction; 'power' is about persistence, meaningless before you have anything to defend.
content.failure_modes: Imagining a moat that doesn't exist yet ('we'll have network effects') as a reason to skip getting customers. Claiming brand/scale with ten users. Using it to feel strategic instead of doing sales.
content.evidence_note: Hamilton Helmer, 'The Foundations of Business Strategy' (2016) — a respected, well-structured synthesis, not peer-reviewed research, and most examples are after-the-fact stories of winners (survivorship bias). Use the categories as a defensibility checklist, not proof your moat holds.
content.examples: You run a scheduling tool for dance studios. More features are easy to copy. But once a studio imports its whole client roster and payment history, leaving is painful — so you invest in deeper data import and integrations rather than flashy features, because switching cost is the one barrier you can actually build at your size.

items.title: Build a loop that feeds itself

content.summary: Instead of chasing one-off wins, find a loop where each step makes the next easier, so effort compounds. Push the loop consistently and it eventually spins on its own momentum.
content.when_to_use: When you have an offer and early sales and want the engine that makes growth repeatable rather than starting from zero each month.
how_to_run: Map your loop: the 3-5 steps where one output becomes the next input (happy customer → referral → new customer → more reviews)
Find the step that, improved, speeds the whole loop
Push that one step hard and consistently
Measure whether the loop turns faster (is each cycle cheaper or bigger?)
Remove anything that breaks the loop, even if it looks productive alone
content.preconditions: You have a real offer and at least early customers, so there's an actual loop to observe rather than an imagined one.
content.failure_modes: Drawing a pretty circular diagram that isn't a real loop: if step C doesn't cause more of step A, it's just a list. Believing it'll 'eventually' spin while you lose customers faster than you add them. A leaky bucket has no flywheel.
content.evidence_note: Popularized by Jim Collins ('Good to Great', 2001); the method is criticized for survivorship bias (picking winners then narrating traits), and several 'great' companies later stumbled. Treat the flywheel as a way to look for compounding loops, not proof a loop guarantees greatness.
content.examples: You sell a paid newsletter and notice the loop: a good issue gets shared → new subscribers → more replies/questions → better material → more shares. The shareable issue drives the loop, so you spend your limited time making each issue worth forwarding rather than buying ads that feed nothing.

items.title: Multiply your output through others

content.summary: Your real output isn't what you personally do; it's what you plus everyone you affect produces. So the highest-value work multiplies many other things: a good hire, a clear process, a decision that unblocks ten tasks.
content.when_to_use: When you're the bottleneck, drowning in tasks, or deciding whether and what to hire or delegate. Most relevant once you have repeatable work worth handing off.
how_to_run: Count your output as the whole operation's, not your to-do list
Find highest-leverage actions: things that affect many tasks (a key hire, a template, a decision others wait on, fixing a recurring fire)
Spend disproportionate time there; drop or automate low-leverage busywork
Before hiring, make the work repeatable and documented
Use short regular check-ins to keep people aligned without doing the work for them
content.preconditions: You have recurring work worth systematizing or handing off. Premature hiring or process before product-market fit usually adds cost and drag.
content.failure_modes: Hiring to feel like a 'real company' before there's repeatable work, burning cash and your time managing them. Elaborate processes for things that should stay scrappy. Confusing busy with high-leverage. Optimizing your task list while the real bottleneck is an unmade decision only you can make.
content.evidence_note: Andy Grove, 'High Output Management' (1983) — widely respected practitioner wisdom; the leverage concept generalizes well, but it was written for managers of established teams, not solo pre-revenue founders, and is experience-based, not experimentally proven. Take the leverage lens; ignore the heavy management machinery until you have a team.
content.examples: A solo founder spends 15 hours a week on manual onboarding and has steady weekly signups. Onboarding is repeatable, so instead of hiring a generalist he spends a week writing a checklist and recording short videos, then hands it to one part-time contractor — freeing the 15 hours for sales, which only he can do well right now.

items.title: Do people actually want this?

content.summary: Product-market fit is when you've built something a specific group genuinely wants, shown by them coming back and being upset if it disappeared. Before that, growth efforts mostly leak out the bottom.
content.when_to_use: Deciding whether to keep iterating or start pushing growth/marketing spend, when sign-ups look fine but usage fades, or when tempted to scale and want a gut-check the core is real.
how_to_run: Look at retention first: of people who tried it, what fraction are still active weeks later, and does the curve flatten instead of dropping to zero?
Ask active users 'how would you feel if you could no longer use this?' and count the share saying 'very disappointed'
Notice pull vs push: are people asking for it and referring others, or are you dragging every user across the line?
Segment ruthlessly: judge fit for the narrow group where it's strongest
If retention is weak, fix the product or narrow the audience; don't spend on growth yet
content.preconditions: You need real users using it over time; you can't measure fit from interviews or sign-up numbers alone.
content.failure_modes: The famous '40% very disappointed' line is a rough benchmark from one practitioner's observations, not a proven threshold — 39% vs 41% is false precision. Founders fake fit with vanity metrics (downloads, waitlist) while retention bleeds, or declare fit from a tiny biased sample. Fit isn't permanent; it can decay.
content.evidence_note: Concept from Marc Andreessen's framing and Steve Blank's work; the 40% survey test is Sean Ellis (~2009-10). The strongest signal — retention/cohort behavior — is well-grounded. The 40% number is folklore-grade: a useful heuristic, not a validated constant. Medium, with the measurement part part-folklore.
content.examples: Your habit-tracking app has 8,000 sign-ups and you're about to spend savings on ads. The data: of people who joined a month ago, 4% still open it. Decision: don't run the ads — that money buys users who also leave. Talk to the 4% who stayed, find what makes them stick, rebuild around that before spending on growth.

items.title: Get out of the building and test your guesses

content.summary: Your business plan is a stack of guesses, not facts. Customer development means going out and testing the riskiest guesses with real people before you waste months building the wrong thing.
content.when_to_use: At idea and early-validation stage, when tempted to build first and ask later, or when you have strong assumptions about who the customer is and what they want but no evidence.
how_to_run: Write your key assumptions: who has the problem, how badly, what they'd pay or do about it
Pick the one assumption that, if wrong, kills the idea, and design a quick test of just that
Talk to real potential customers about their problem (not your solution); ask what they do today, not whether they'd 'like' your idea
Look for evidence of action (paid, searched, hacked a workaround, asked), not politeness
Update or pivot, then test the next risky assumption
content.preconditions: You must be willing to hear 'no' and change course; it only works if you let evidence override your plan.
content.failure_modes: Fake interviews that fish for compliments ('Would you use this? Cool!'), mistaking politeness for demand. People are bad at predicting their own behavior, so stated interest evaporates at the payment step. Can be used to stall forever, talking instead of shipping or asking for money.
content.evidence_note: Formalized by Steve Blank, extended by Eric Ries (Lean Startup) and Rob Fitzpatrick (The Mom Test) for interview technique. Widely taught and battle-tested; not a controlled result but strongly grounded in repeated experience. Medium, with the caveat that interview quality decides whether it helps or misleads.
content.examples: About to spend three months coding a tutor marketplace, you instead spend a week messaging 15 parents and 15 tutors about how they find each other today. You learn tutors already have all the students they want via word of mouth — the real pain is parents not trusting strangers. Decision: don't build the marketplace; test whether a trust/vetting service is what people pay for.

items.title: Sell by asking the right questions, in order

content.summary: For considered purchases you sell better by asking questions than by pitching. Walk the buyer through their situation, the problem, what it's costing them, and the value of fixing it — so they talk themselves into needing it.
content.when_to_use: Talking to potential buyers for a considered purchase, running discovery calls or demos, or trying to land first paying customers when your pitch keeps falling flat.
how_to_run: Situation: a few quick facts about how they work today (keep short — too many bore buyers)
Problem: what's frustrating, hard, or unreliable about the current way
Implication: what that problem costs them, knock-on effects, what continuing means
Need-payoff: what it'd be worth solved, so they state the value in their own words
Only then present your solution mapped to the needs they described, and listen
content.preconditions: Best for considered/B2B-style or higher-stakes purchases; for tiny impulse buys it overcomplicates.
content.failure_modes: Done mechanically it feels like an interrogation; too many 'situation' questions bore buyers (the research itself flags this). It can't save a product nobody needs — good questions on a worthless offer just expose the lack of value faster. Misused to manufacture urgency about a non-problem, which erodes trust.
content.evidence_note: Neil Rackham/Huthwaite, from observing ~35,000 sales calls across 23 countries over ~12 years, coding behaviors against outcomes — the largest empirical study of selling ever; Implication questions correlated strongly with larger closes. High, genuinely evidence-backed, though the data is decades old and skews large B2B.
content.examples: A technical founder terrified of 'selling' stops pitching and asks: how do you handle this today? where does it break down? what does it cost when it breaks? what would it mean if it were gone? The buyer says 'this wastes about a day a week' — now you know your price anchor and value message, and they ask to buy rather than being pushed.

items.title: Teach the buyer something, then guide the deal

content.summary: Instead of just being friendly and asking what the buyer wants, teach them a fresh insight about their own business, tailor it to them, and confidently guide the conversation, including price. Buyers value a seller who shows them something they didn't know.
content.when_to_use: When stuck competing on price, when buyers don't grasp why you're different, or when deals stall because the customer doesn't see the problem clearly enough to act.
how_to_run: Find an insight: something true and surprising about their situation or cost-of-inaction they haven't fully seen
Lead with that teaching point, framing the problem before mentioning your solution
Tailor it to this buyer's world and the people in the decision
Take gentle control: push back where fair, talk price directly, steer toward a decision
content.preconditions: Works best in complex/considered sales where you genuinely have a non-obvious insight; if you have nothing real to teach, it backfires.
content.failure_modes: Without a genuine insight, 'teaching' becomes condescending lecturing. 'Taking control' easily tips into pushy or arrogant, especially damaging for a first-time founder still building trust. The model's headline claim has been contested.
content.evidence_note: Dixon & Adamson (CEB, 2011), from a survey of thousands of reps finding 'Challenger' types over-represented among top performers in complex sales — real but correlational and self-reported, and later analyses questioned how strong and general the effect is. A useful, popular framework, but not as proven as often sold. Medium.
content.examples: You keep losing an analytics tool to 'we'll just use spreadsheets.' Instead of listing features, you open with a number: 'Teams your size lose ~6 hours a week reconciling spreadsheet versions, and one bad number in a board deck can cost a funding round.' That reframes 'free spreadsheets' as expensive — then you introduce the tool as the fix.

items.title: Hire against a written scorecard with structured interviews

content.summary: Before you interview anyone, write exactly what the job must achieve and how you'll score it; then ask every candidate the same job-related questions and rate their real past results. This beats gut-feel hiring because you compare people against the job, not your mood.
content.when_to_use: About to make your first few hires (or first contractor) where a wrong choice is expensive and slow to undo. Best once the role's real work is concrete enough to describe.
how_to_run: Write a one-page scorecard: why the role exists, 3-6 outcomes for the first 6-12 months, competencies needed
Define the same 4-6 job-related questions and a 1-3 scale for every candidate
Interview for past behavior ('walk me through how you actually did X'), pushing for specifics and numbers
Score each candidate against the scorecard right after, before discussing with anyone
Reference-check against the same outcomes; hire only when evidence clears the bar
content.preconditions: You can articulate the role's concrete outcomes. If you can't describe what success looks like, you're not ready to hire for it.
content.failure_modes: Applying it before the role is defined produces a scorecard for a job that doesn't exist yet. Fake-structure: same questions but scoring on vibes, or rewriting the bar to fit someone you already like. The famous '90% success' claim is the authors' self-reported number, not audited. No process removes bias entirely.
content.evidence_note: Scorecard + structured interviews popularized by Smart & Street ('Who'), building on Topgrading. The branded success claims are self-reported (folklore-grade), but the load-bearing idea — structured, job-related interviews with consistent scoring — has strong independent meta-analytic support (e.g., Sackett et al. 2022, ~0.42 validity). Structure = high; branding = medium.
content.regional_notes: Reference-checking norms and legal limits vary by country (age, family, salary-history questions are restricted in some places). Keep questions strictly job-related and check local employment law.
content.examples: A founder needs a first salesperson. Instead of hiring the most charismatic person, they write a scorecard ('close 8 of our $2k/mo plans in 90 days, build a repeatable demo script') and ask all three finalists 'walk me through the last deal you closed start to finish.' The smooth talker has never run a full cycle; the quieter candidate has. They hire on the evidence.

items.title: A small team of strong performers beats a big team of average ones

content.summary: A few excellent people who raise each other's game usually beat a larger group where some are weak, because weak performers quietly drag down strong ones. Hire slowly, pay well for real talent, and don't keep someone just to fill a seat.
content.when_to_use: Deciding whether to add headcount, keep a mediocre hire, or 'throw bodies' at a problem. Especially once you have a working offer and need to grow deliberately.
how_to_run: Default to the smallest team that can do the work; add a person only when work clearly exceeds capacity
Set a high bar per hire; resist hiring fast to feel productive
Address a clear underperformer quickly and honestly rather than working around them for months
Reinvest money saved on headcount into paying strong people well
Re-check: would I enthusiastically re-hire each person for their current role?
content.preconditions: You can attract strong candidates and the work rewards skill over raw volume of hands.
content.failure_modes: Misused as license to overwork a skeleton crew, churn through 'low performers' you never managed, or create a fearful culture where people hide problems. Survivorship-flavored: the famous example is one already-successful, cash-rich company in a winner-take-most market. Firing is one-way, human, and often legally regulated.
content.evidence_note: Popularized by Reed Hastings/Netflix ('No Rules Rules') — coherent philosophy resting largely on one company's self-told story, partly folklore and survivorship-prone. The narrower claim (a weak member lowers strong ones; small senior teams can outperform larger junior ones) has some team-research support; the 'fire generously, pay top-of-market' prescription isn't proven to generalize. Medium.
content.regional_notes: 'Pay top-of-market and part quickly' assumes at-will employment and big salary budgets; in many countries termination is legally slow/costly, so a 'swap freely' approach is impractical and harmful. Adjust to local norms.
content.examples: A founder on a small budget is tempted to hire two cheap junior devs to ship faster. Reframing around talent density, they hire one experienced engineer they'd be thrilled to keep, accept slightly slower early output, and find fewer coordination headaches and bugs make the team faster within a couple of months.

items.title: Feedback that both cares about the person and says the hard thing

content.summary: Good feedback does two things at once: shows you genuinely care about the person, and says the difficult thing clearly instead of dancing around it. Only nice is useless; only blunt is cruel — you need both.
content.when_to_use: Once you manage anyone — first hire, contractor, or co-founder — and need praise or criticism that actually changes behavior. Most useful for the recurring small conversations, not just formal reviews.
how_to_run: Get clear on what you want to change and that you're saying it because you want them to succeed
Say the specific thing directly and soon, with a concrete example, not a vague label
Make it about a behavior and its impact, not their character
Ask for their view and listen — you may be missing context
Agree what changes next; give praise just as specifically when it goes right
content.preconditions: There's an ongoing relationship where feedback can lead to change. For one-off vendors it matters less.
content.failure_modes: Most common abuse: treating it as permission to be harsh ('I'm just being direct') while skipping the 'care' half — obnoxious aggression dressed as candor. Opposite: staying so 'nice' the message never lands. Not a script for insults, and not a substitute for a fair process when a job is actually at risk.
content.evidence_note: Framework popularized by Kim Scott ('Radical Candor': care personally + challenge directly), from management experience, not controlled studies — the 2x2 is practitioner folklore. The components are better supported: feedback research finds specific, behavior-focused, timely feedback beats vague/delayed, while ego-threatening feedback backfires. Medium.
content.regional_notes: Directness norms vary a lot: what reads as honest-and-caring in the US or Netherlands can feel rude in Japan or much of East Asia, and too indirect elsewhere. Calibrate how direct to the person and culture.
content.examples: A founder's only contractor keeps missing deadlines, and the founder has silently re-done the work to avoid the awkward chat. Care-and-challenge: 'I rely on you and want this to keep working — the last two builds were three days late with no heads-up and blocked the launch. What's going on, and what do we change?' Turns out a tooling problem nobody flagged; they fix it and deadlines hold.

items.title: Does one customer make or lose you money?
triggers: do i make money per customer

content.summary: Strip the business down to one customer: what you earn from them minus the cost to serve them, versus what it cost to win them, and how long until that money comes back. If a single customer loses money, getting more just loses money faster.
content.when_to_use: Once you're charging real money and want to know if the model works before pouring time or cash into growth. To sanity-check pricing, channels, and whether 'we just need more users' is actually true.
how_to_run: Compute contribution margin: price minus variable costs to deliver (not whole overhead)
CAC: everything spent to acquire customers in a period divided by customers won (include ads, your time, tools, fees)
Estimate LTV from gross margin and real observed retention — not hoped-for — then compare LTV to CAC
Payback: how many months of margin to earn CAC back; shorter is safer
If a customer loses money, fix margin, price, or acquisition cost before scaling
content.preconditions: You have at least some real transactions and cost data. With zero sales these are guesses; label them as guesses.
content.failure_modes: LTV fantasy math: assuming 5-year retention off 3 months of data, using revenue instead of gross margin, leaving your own labor and fees out of CAC. A 'great' 4:1 ratio on invented retention is worse than a modest 2.5:1 on real cohorts. Obsessing over a blended average that hides one profitable and one bleeding channel.
content.evidence_note: Standard accounting/finance constructs (contribution margin, CAC, LTV, payback) — the math is high-evidence and uncontroversial; the danger is entirely in the inputs. Benchmarks like '3:1 LTV:CAC' or 'under 12-month payback' are useful rules of thumb but folklore, not laws.
content.regional_notes: Payment fees, refund/chargeback rates, taxes (VAT/GST) and typical payback expectations differ by market and currency; compute margin on what you actually net locally.
content.examples: A founder selling a $30/month app feels great as signups climb. Unit economics: each customer costs $90 in ads, churns after 4 months (~$120 revenue, ~$96 margin at 80% gross) — roughly break-even with a fragile 3-4 month payback. Decision: pause paid ads, fix early churn first, rather than raising money to 'scale' a model that barely works.

items.title: Where should the next dollar and next hour go?
triggers: should i spend on ads or product

content.summary: You have limited money and limited hours, so the weekly question is: of all the things I could put the next dollar or hour into, which gives the best return for the risk? Treat your own time as your scarcest capital, not just cash.
content.when_to_use: Once real options compete for the same money or time — ads vs product, hiring vs building, this feature vs that channel. Most useful once you have some revenue signal to compare against.
how_to_run: List the live options competing for the next chunk of money/time and what each could return
Estimate return-per-dollar and return-per-hour, and how reversible each bet is
Fund the few highest-expected-return, hardest-to-undo-later options first; treat cheap reversible bets as experiments
Set a small budget/time cap and a checkpoint date for each bet, then actually review
Cut or shrink what's underperforming and move that capital to what's working — don't let sunk cost hold it
content.preconditions: There are genuinely competing uses of resource and at least rough signal on returns. Pre-revenue the comparison is cruder but the discipline still helps.
content.failure_modes: Over-modeling: spreadsheets implying false precision about returns you can't predict. Opposite: 'spray and pray' across ten things so nothing gets enough to work. Sunk-cost thinking ('we've already put 3 months in') keeps dead bets alive. Forgetting your hours are the scarcest capital and burning yourself out. Copying a big company's playbook ignores buffers a first-time founder doesn't have.
content.evidence_note: A long-standing, well-grounded finance concept (opportunity cost, expected return, marginal allocation) — the principle is solid. Applying it to an early startup with uncertain returns is judgment, not science: inputs are guesses, so it's a thinking discipline, not a precise optimizer. The 'allocate like a great CEO' version leans on survivorship stories. Medium.
content.examples: A founder has $5,000 and ~20 free hours a week and wants to do everything. Treating it as capital allocation: ads have a measurable short payback (reversible, stop anytime), the new feature is a big irreversible time sink with unclear demand, content is slow. They put $4,000 into ads with a 3-week kill-switch, spend hours validating the feature with customers before building, and shelve content — reallocating based on what the ad test shows.

items.title: Attack where the big players won't fight back

content.summary: Win a foothold by serving customers the established players ignore or overserve, with a simpler or cheaper offer they have little incentive to defend, then improve from there. Compete where the response won't come, not head-on.
content.when_to_use: You're small or solo facing entrenched, well-resourced players and can't win feature-for-feature; you've spotted a segment the big players underserve or find unprofitable.
how_to_run: Map who the dominant players actively serve and which customers they over- or under-serve
Pick a beachhead unattractive for an incumbent to defend
Build a simpler/cheaper offer that's good enough for that segment, not a clone
Win that segment first, then improve up-market
Watch whether the leaders respond; if they ignore you, the wedge is working
content.preconditions: There is a real segment established players neglect, and you can reach it affordably.
content.failure_modes: Treating any cheap product as 'disruptive' — most low-end products just lose. Weak at prediction: easy to label a winner in hindsight. A 'good enough' product that never improves stays stuck; a beachhead with no path up-market is just a small low-margin business. Sometimes the incumbent does fight back.
content.evidence_note: From Clayton Christensen ('The Innovator's Dilemma', 1997). Influential but graded medium honestly: the descriptive pattern is well-observed, predictive power contested (Jill Lepore's 2014 critique; later reviews found the founding hard-drive cases were selectively read). A lens for finding an unguarded entry point, not a law that low-end entrants win.
content.regional_notes: Travels well to emerging/price-sensitive markets where a simpler, cheaper, mobile-first offer reaches customers global leaders price out.
content.examples: A solo founder can't out-feature the dominant project tool used by big teams, so they build a stripped-down cheap tool for freelancers who find the leader bloated — a group the leader won't chase — and grow from there.

items.title: Ship the smallest test, measure, learn, repeat
triggers: am i building the right thing
is anyone going to use this

content.summary: Instead of building the full product on a hunch, ship the smallest thing that tests your riskiest assumption, measure how real users react, and use that to decide whether to keep going or change course. Shorten the loop from idea to evidence.
content.when_to_use: Pre-product or early, unsure anyone wants it, tempted to build for months in the dark; you have risky assumptions you could test cheaply.
how_to_run: Name the single riskiest assumption (usually 'someone wants this enough to act')
Design the smallest test/MVP that produces real evidence on it
Decide in advance what result means continue vs change
Put it in front of real users; measure behavior, not opinions
Decide: persevere, adjust, or pivot — then run the next loop
content.preconditions: You can reach real potential users and define a concrete success signal before building.
content.failure_modes: 'MVPs' that are just unfinished products with no learning goal. Vanity metrics (signups, likes) instead of real demand behavior. Endless iterating with no kill criteria. Over-applying where the answer is known or where a shoddy first version destroys trust. 'Pivot' used to justify chasing every shiny idea.
content.evidence_note: Eric Ries ('The Lean Startup', 2011), building on customer development. Medium: the logic (test cheaply, prefer evidence) is sound and broadly endorsed, but little rigorous causal proof it raises success rates; much supporting material is anecdote. Disciplined hypothesis-testing, not a guaranteed path.
content.regional_notes: Fast where ads/landing-page tests are cheap; where users are offline, the 'measure' step needs in-person or messaging-app tests.
content.examples: A technical solo founder wanting to build an AI invoicing tool first puts up a one-page site with a 'request access' button, sends it to 40 small-business owners, and watches who signs up — learning what to lead with before writing real code.

items.title: Stop competing on the usual things; change the game

content.summary: Rather than beating rivals at what everyone competes on, redraw the offer: drop or reduce things the industry takes for granted, add new value customers actually want, ideally lowering cost too. Make the usual competition beside the point.
content.when_to_use: Entering a crowded, look-alike market where everyone competes on the same dimensions and head-on competition looks like a losing race.
how_to_run: List every factor the industry competes on and assumes is mandatory
For each ask: eliminate, reduce, raise, or create something new?
Design an offer dropping costly things customers don't value and adding new value they do
Check the new mix is deliverable at a cost that works
Aim at people the industry currently ignores, not just existing buyers
content.preconditions: You understand what customers truly value vs what the industry assumes, and can deliver a different mix.
content.failure_modes: Far easier to describe a 'new space' after the fact than create one on purpose. Cutting features customers actually need just makes a worse product. A new mix can be copied fast if not protected. Founders rationalize a no-demand niche by calling it 'uncontested' — empty space is often empty because nobody wants what's there.
content.evidence_note: Kim & Mauborgne ('Blue Ocean Strategy', 2005). Popular as a framing tool; graded low on hard evidence — supporting cases are retrospective and selected, heavy survivorship bias, no proof the method reliably produces winners. Use to challenge assumptions and generate options, not as proof a reinvented offer will work.
content.regional_notes: Useful where a global incumbent's model carries costly assumptions that don't fit local needs.
content.examples: A founder eyeing the crowded online-course market notices everyone competes on huge video libraries, so they drop the giant catalog and long lectures and add live weekly accountability and a tight 30-day path — aimed at busy people who never finish courses.

items.title: Name and own a new category, don't just sell a product
triggers: explain my product in one line
be the leader not a me-too
what box do i fit in

content.summary: Instead of positioning as a better option inside an existing category, define a new problem and category, name it, and become the reference point for it. The bet: whoever frames the category captures most of its value.
content.when_to_use: You genuinely solve a problem with no clean label yet, and being compared inside an existing category makes you look like a weaker copy of an established leader.
how_to_run: Articulate a problem the market doesn't name clearly and why old solutions fall short
Name the new category in plain language; define what's in and out
Frame messaging around the problem and category, not features
Educate the market consistently so the category and your role stick
Build the product to credibly deliver on the category you claim
content.preconditions: A real underserved problem existing categories describe poorly, and stamina to educate a market.
content.failure_modes: Highly survivorship-prone: 'category king' stories are told backward from winners, ignoring the many that named a category and went nowhere. Naming doesn't create demand; if the problem is weak, a clever label adds confusion and a longer sales cycle. Founders invent jargon for a product nobody asked for. Market education is expensive and slow — brutal for an under-funded solo founder.
content.evidence_note: Associated with 'Play Bigger' (2016). Influential in marketing circles; graded low — retrospective case studies of winners, heavy survivorship bias, no controlled comparison. A positioning lens, not proof that inventing a category causes success.
content.regional_notes: Market-education cost is far higher where you must educate across multiple languages or low-trust markets; easier in a tight niche community.
content.examples: A founder whose software sits between recruiting and onboarding finds buyers keep mis-filing them as 'just another HR tool' and comparing on price. They coin a plain-language name for the in-between problem and lead with it — accepting more time spent explaining before anyone buys.

items.title: Check a deal is real before pouring time into it

content.summary: Before investing heavily in an opportunity, confirm what makes a deal closeable: a measurable payoff, who controls the money, how they decide and buy, a real pain worth solving, and an internal advocate. Missing pieces are things to go find, not ignore.
content.when_to_use: Selling a considered/higher-priced product (often B2B) with multiple people involved; deals stall and you can't tell which are real.
how_to_run: For each deal, write the measurable result the buyer gets and the pain driving it
Identify who controls the budget and who else must say yes
Map how this org decides and approves, and on what timeline
Find and develop a champion who wants you to win and can sell internally
Treat any blank as the next thing to uncover; if too many stay blank, deprioritize
content.preconditions: You have real conversations with the buying side, not outside guesswork.
content.failure_modes: Turning it into an interrogation that annoys buyers. Filling boxes with assumptions, producing false confidence. Over-applying to small self-serve purchases. Mistaking a friendly contact for a real champion, or a 'nice-to-have' for a funded pain.
content.evidence_note: MEDDIC originated in 1990s enterprise software sales (PTC; Dunkel & Napoli), widely used in B2B. Medium: practitioner-proven and broadly adopted with strong face validity, but support is professional consensus and internal results, not independent controlled studies.
content.regional_notes: Decision processes and number of approvers vary by region and company size; relationship and hierarchy weigh heavily in some markets, so re-learn 'how they decide' per market.
content.examples: A solo founder has five demos that 'went great' but none closed. Going deal by deal, they realize in four they never reached the budget holder or confirmed a funded problem — so they refocus on the one deal with a champion, a clear payoff, and a known approval path.

items.title: Find the one leaky step and fix it first
triggers: people sign up but don't come back

content.summary: Break the journey into stages — get them in, to first value, back again, referring, and paying — measure each, and concentrate on the single weakest stage rather than everything at once. Fixing the biggest leak gives the biggest return.
content.when_to_use: You have some users/traffic and growth feels stuck, but you're not sure where it's breaking and you're spreading effort thin.
how_to_run: Define each funnel stage and what counts as passing it
Measure conversion from each stage to the next so drop-offs show
Identify the single worst or most damaging leak (early stages gate everything after)
Run focused experiments on just that stage until it improves
Re-measure, then move to the next-weakest stage
content.preconditions: You can track real behavior across stages — even rough analytics or a spreadsheet of cohort counts.
content.failure_modes: Optimizing a late stage while an earlier one is the real leak. Pouring money into acquisition to mask poor retention — filling a bucket with holes. Chasing vanity totals instead of stage-to-stage conversion. Treating the five stages as rigid when your journey differs. Reading noise as signal with tiny numbers.
content.evidence_note: AARRR ('pirate metrics'), Dave McClure ~2007 — a practical framework, not a research result. Medium: widely adopted, intuitive, operationally sound for prioritizing, but a heuristic with no formal validation; the five labels are convention.
content.regional_notes: Acquisition and referral behavior differ sharply by region/platform (messaging-app sharing vs link referral), so measure those stages locally.
content.examples: A founder pays for ads and signups climb but revenue stays flat. Mapping the funnel, acquisition and signup are fine but most users never reach their first real result — so they stop buying ads and rebuild first-run onboarding, lifting every downstream stage.

items.title: Make it safe to speak up and admit mistakes

content.summary: When people believe they won't be punished or humiliated for speaking up, admitting errors, or asking questions, the team learns faster and catches problems sooner. Your job is to make that belief true.
content.when_to_use: As you bring on first hires/contractors and need them to flag bad news, risks, and mistakes early instead of hiding them until too late.
how_to_run: React to the first bad-news report or mistake with curiosity, not punishment
Ask for dissent explicitly and go last so your view doesn't anchor everyone
Frame the work as uncertain so admitting 'I don't know' becomes normal
Thank people by name for raising risks, questions, or errors
content.preconditions: You have at least one other person whose candor affects outcomes.
content.failure_modes: Treating it as 'being nice' and avoiding hard feedback, lowering standards instead of raising candor; punishing the messenger once under stress, which silences everyone; confusing safety with consensus and never deciding.
content.evidence_note: Amy Edmondson's peer-reviewed 1999 study (Administrative Science Quarterly, 51 teams) found psychological safety predicts team learning — genuine peer-reviewed support, unusual for management concepts.
