The famous corporate 'top factor' finding that popularized it was observational (no proven causal direction). Background only; never name the source to the founder.
content.regional_notes: In higher power-distance cultures juniors may stay silent regardless, so actively invite input rather than just leaving the door open.
content.examples: A founder's first part-time engineer spots a broken data sync before a demo but stays quiet fearing they'll look incompetent; the demo fails live. Afterward the founder opens each check-in with 'what's the scariest thing you saw this week?' and thanks the engineer the next time they flag a problem early.

items.title: Give every task and decision exactly one owner
triggers: we both thought the other would

content.summary: Every task and decision should have exactly one person accountable for it getting done. If two people are accountable, in practice no one is.
content.when_to_use: Work slips through the cracks, you and a co-founder/early hires have fuzzy or overlapping roles, or decisions stall because it's unclear who calls it.
how_to_run: For each important task/decision, write the single name accountable for the outcome
Separate 'accountable' (one person) from 'consulted' and 'informed' (can be several)
When two people both feel responsible, force a choice so one name remains
Revisit ownership when roles change so nothing is orphaned or doubly owned
content.failure_modes: Confusing accountable with 'does all the work' so the owner feels dumped on; assigning an owner who lacks authority or information to deliver; using it to diffuse blame rather than clarify who drives; over-documenting roles for a two-person team where a conversation would do.
content.evidence_note: Practitioner wisdom, not peer-reviewed: the 'directly responsible individual' practice is associated with well-known tech operating cultures, and the responsibility-matrix idea (taught as RACI) comes from project-management bodies of knowledge. Widely adopted but case-based, not experimental.
content.examples: Two co-founders each assume the other is handling the company's first tax registration; neither follows up and they miss a deadline and pay a penalty. They fix it with a one-line list: each recurring obligation gets exactly one founder's name, even if the other helps.

items.title: Prefer bets that lose little but could win big

content.summary: Favor bets where the worst case costs a little but the best case pays off a lot, and avoid bets where a bad outcome could wipe you out even if the upside is tempting. Cap the downside, keep the upside open.
content.when_to_use: Deciding how much time/money to put behind an uncertain idea, choosing between experiments, or tempted to go all-in on a single unproven bet.
how_to_run: For each option, write the realistic worst case and best case in plain terms
Rule out anything whose worst case could end the company, however good the upside
Prefer cheap, time-boxed tests that preserve the chance of a big payoff
Run several small asymmetric bets rather than one large symmetric one
content.failure_modes: Calling something a 'small bet' while ignoring hidden downside (reputation, legal, opportunity cost); taking so many tiny bets none gets enough to pay off; using 'optionality' to never commit; underestimating the real odds of the catastrophic case.
content.evidence_note: Practitioner/trader wisdom (the 'barbell' approach), rooted in real option math but the startup application is heuristic, not validated. Warn flag because founders routinely mislabel genuinely ruinous bets as 'small.'
content.regional_notes: Where capital is hard to raise and the safety net thin, the downside of a ruinous bet is harsher, so capping downside matters even more.
content.examples: A founder can spend six months and their savings building a full product for an unproven idea, or two weekends and a small ad budget on a fake landing page to test demand. The landing page risks little but could reveal real demand, so they run that first.

items.title: Judge the decision, not just how it turned out
triggers: did i make the right choice

content.summary: A good decision can still have a bad outcome, and a bad decision can get lucky. Judge the quality of how you decided given what you knew at the time, not just the result.
content.when_to_use: Reviewing a decision that went badly or surprisingly well, tempted to overhaul your whole approach after one bad break, or deciding what to actually learn from a win or loss.
how_to_run: Separate the decision (what you chose and why) from the outcome (what happened)
Ask whether the information and reasoning were sound given what you knew then, not now
Keep a quick written record of why you decided, to review honestly later
Update your process only for genuine reasoning errors, not for outcomes that were mostly luck
content.failure_modes: Using 'I just got unlucky' to dodge real reasoning errors; overcorrecting after one bad outcome that was actually a fine decision; hindsight bias rewriting what you 'must have known'; over-analyzing process until you're slow and indecisive.
content.evidence_note: Practitioner wisdom from decision-science and professional-poker writing ('resulting'), drawing on well-studied hindsight bias. The biases are well-studied; the founder application is heuristic. Warn flag because it's easily abused to excuse poor decisions.
content.examples: A founder picks the cheaper payment provider after a quick comparison; weeks later it has an outage during a sale. Before concluding 'I chose wrong,' they ask whether that was foreseeable then. It wasn't, so they keep their selection process and just add a backup provider rather than distrusting every past choice.

items.title: Strategy is choosing what not to do

content.summary: Strategy is deciding what you will deliberately NOT do, and building a tight set of choices that reinforce each other for one kind of customer. If you're not giving anything up, you're not really choosing.
content.when_to_use: Tempted to serve everyone, chasing every feature request or market, or struggling to explain why a customer should pick you over a generic competitor.
how_to_run: Pick the specific customer and need you'll serve best, and name who you are NOT for
List what you will deliberately not do or offer, and accept the customers you'll lose
Check your key choices reinforce each other rather than pulling apart
Reject requests and opportunities that don't fit, even attractive ones
content.preconditions: Enough contact with real customers to know which segment and need you can serve distinctively.
content.failure_modes: Mistaking operational improvements (faster, cheaper, better) for strategy when they offer no real trade-off; writing a 'focus' statement but still saying yes to everything; copying a competitor's positioning so no genuine choice is made; cutting so much there's no viable market left.
content.evidence_note: The trade-offs-and-fit view comes from a widely cited 1996 strategy article plus decades of teaching — the most academically grounded of the practitioner concepts here, but conceptual/case-based rather than experimentally tested. Medium. Never name the author to the founder.
content.examples: A founder building a scheduling tool keeps adding features for freelancers, agencies, and clinics at once, and it feels generic to all three. They choose to serve only solo therapists, drop agency features, and tune onboarding, pricing, and language for that group; conversion improves because every choice points the same way.

items.title: Spending on growth before retention is proven

content.summary: Confirm people actually stick before spending to grow — scaling a leaky bucket just loses money faster.
content.when_to_use: You're tempted to spend on acquisition but aren't sure users retain.
how_to_run: Pull your cohort retention curve and check it flattens instead of falling to zero
Run the 'how disappointed would you be if this disappeared?' check with active users this week
Only spend on growth once retention holds
content.failure_modes: Buying users who don't come back; mistaking signups for fit.

items.title: Accepting an 'industry standard' cost or assumption

content.summary: Rebuild the number from what's actually, physically true rather than copying convention.
content.when_to_use: Something looks expensive or fixed because 'that's how the industry does it.'
how_to_run: Write the 3 irreducible cost (or requirement) drivers of your product
Delete every assumption that's just convention, not a real constraint
Rebuild the cheapest path that still meets the real constraints
content.failure_modes: Mislabeling a real constraint as a convention and building something that can't work.

items.title: Discounting to hit a number, training customers to wait

content.summary: Protect price and sell value; repeated discounts teach customers the real price is the discount.
content.when_to_use: You keep discounting to close deals or hit a target.
how_to_run: Replace your next discount with a quantified ROI statement in the customer's own numbers
Hold the price and ask what outcome would make it clearly worth it
content.failure_modes: Permanent margin erosion; attracting price-shoppers who churn.

items.title: Keeping a 'fine' performer who drives the best people away

content.summary: Defend talent density and act early; tolerating a weak performer quietly costs you your strong ones.
content.when_to_use: You're working around someone who's 'okay' but not great.
how_to_run: Apply the keeper test: would you fight to keep this person if they tried to leave?
Start the honest conversation you've been avoiding this week
content.failure_modes: Letting it drag for months while your best people disengage; acting harshly without managing first.

items.title: Launching broad and becoming nobody's first choice

content.summary: Win one narrow niche completely before expanding; a sharp message to a specific buyer beats a vague one to all.
content.when_to_use: Your product feels generic and isn't anyone's obvious first pick.
how_to_run: Name the smallest segment you could be #1 in within 6 months
Refocus product, message, and outreach on only that group
content.failure_modes: Diluting the message across segments so it resonates with no one.

items.title: Forecasting deals with no champion or budget owner

content.summary: Qualify on who controls the money and who's championing you before committing time to a deal.
content.when_to_use: Deals look promising but stall or go silent.
how_to_run: Score your top 3 deals on: do you have a champion AND an economic buyer?
Demote the ones missing both and reinvest the time in real ones
content.failure_modes: Mistaking a friendly contact for a real champion; a polite 'maybe' treated as pipeline.

items.title: Building for a long time before anyone wanted it

content.summary: Test the riskiest assumption with the smallest possible artifact before committing months of build.
content.when_to_use: You've been heads-down building with little real user contact.
how_to_run: Ship the smallest thing that proves someone wants this (a landing page, a manual delivery)
Define the exact metric that would confirm demand, and watch real behavior
content.failure_modes: Spending the runway to build the wrong thing; counting compliments as demand.

items.title: Agonizing over a reversible call, rushing an irreversible one

content.summary: Tag the door type first: reversible decisions should be fast; irreversible ones deserve a slow, careful look.
content.when_to_use: You're stuck on a decision and unsure how much deliberation it deserves.
how_to_run: Label each pending decision one-way (hard to undo) or two-way (easy to undo)
Fast-track every two-way door now; reserve real deliberation for one-way doors
content.failure_modes: Spending weeks on a cheap reversible test while rushing a permanent commitment.

items.title: Hiring on charisma and résumé, misfit on outcomes

content.summary: Score candidates against the role's concrete outcomes, not how impressive they seem.
content.when_to_use: You're about to hire and leaning on gut impression.
how_to_run: Write 3-5 outcomes the role must hit in 6-12 months before your next interview
Ask every candidate to walk through how they actually did similar things; grade against the outcomes
content.failure_modes: A charismatic hire who can't deliver the actual job; rewriting the bar to fit someone you like.

items.title: Optimizing acquisition while activation/retention are broken

content.summary: Fix the weakest funnel stage first — early leaks (activation, retention) make everything downstream pointless.
content.when_to_use: You're pushing for more top-of-funnel but conversion or retention is weak.
how_to_run: Put a number on each funnel stage (acquisition → activation → retention → referral → revenue)
Attack the worst stage first before spending more on acquisition
content.failure_modes: Pouring money into the top of a leaky funnel.

items.title: Entering a structurally brutal industry, blaming yourself for thin margins

content.summary: Check the industry structure before committing more — some markets cap profit no matter how well you execute.
content.when_to_use: Margins are thin and you're wondering if it's you or the market.
how_to_run: Rate the five forces (rivalry, new entrants, substitutes, buyer power, supplier power) high/low for your market
Decide whether your specific wedge can still win, or whether to pick a better-structured market
content.failure_modes: Blaming execution for a structural margin problem; treating a 'bad' industry as a stop sign when a real wedge exists.

items.title: Running on too little runway, one miss from fatal

content.summary: Keep a real margin of safety; plan to the case where things go ~30% worse than your best estimate.
content.when_to_use: Your cash plan only works if everything goes right.
how_to_run: Re-forecast a '30% worse' case (slower revenue, later close)
Rebuild a cash buffer or cut costs so a normal miss isn't fatal
content.failure_modes: Planning to exactly your best-case runway; no buffer for ordinary slippage.

items.title: Sugarcoating feedback until it becomes a termination

content.summary: Give candor early and specifically; withholding the hard thing to be 'nice' is actually unkind.
content.when_to_use: You've been postponing direct feedback to keep the peace.
how_to_run: Deliver the one piece of direct, specific feedback you've been putting off — today
Name the behavior and its impact, then agree what changes
content.failure_modes: Silence that builds until the only option left is firing.

items.title: Chasing the biggest market where you have no right to win

content.summary: Weigh market attractiveness against your actual edge; pick where both are high, not just where the market is big.
content.when_to_use: You're drawn to a large market but unsure you can win it.
how_to_run: Score candidate markets on two axes: attractiveness AND your unfair advantage
Pick the high-high one, even if smaller
content.failure_modes: Entering a giant market with no edge and losing to focused competitors.

items.title: Diffuse ownership; work falls through the cracks

content.summary: Assign exactly one accountable owner per thing — if two people are accountable, no one is.
content.when_to_use: Tasks slip because it's unclear who owns them.
how_to_run: For your current project, list each workstream with exactly one accountable name
Make sure that person has the authority and info to actually deliver
content.failure_modes: 'We both thought the other had it'; diffusing blame instead of clarifying who drives.

items.title: Pitching the product before establishing a painful, quantified gap

content.summary: Diagnose before prescribing — make the buyer feel the cost of their current situation before you present the solution.
content.when_to_use: Your pitch isn't landing and buyers don't see why they need you.
how_to_run: For your top deal, write the buyer's current state, desired state, and the cost of the gap between them
Lead the next conversation with that gap, not your features
content.failure_modes: Presenting features at someone who hasn't admitted a problem worth paying to solve.

items.title: Reinvesting in low-return work out of habit

content.summary: Allocate the next dollar and hour to its best available use, not to whatever you did last time.
content.when_to_use: You keep funding the same activities without checking the return.
how_to_run: List every option for the next dollar/hour and rank by expected return
Shift resources to the top one; cut or shrink the laggards
content.failure_modes: Sunk-cost and habit keeping money in low-return work.

items.title: Spreading investment evenly so nothing becomes a strength
triggers: cant decide what to double down on

content.summary: Concentrate on the one or two capabilities that are both valuable and hard to copy; over-invest there.
content.when_to_use: You're doing a little of everything and nothing stands out.
how_to_run: Identify the 1-2 capabilities that are valuable AND hard for others to copy
Over-invest in those; deliberately under-invest elsewhere
content.failure_modes: Even spreading that produces no durable advantage anywhere.

items.title: Hiding problems in a blame culture until they explode

content.summary: Build psychological safety so problems surface early; punishing the messenger guarantees you hear bad news too late.
content.when_to_use: People around you don't flag problems until they're crises.
how_to_run: Open your next meeting by naming one of your own mistakes, to model it
Thank the next person who raises a risk or error early, by name
content.failure_modes: Reacting to bad news with blame, which silences everyone permanently.

items.title: Treating growth as a funnel you keep refilling with paid spend

content.summary: Build a compounding loop where each user generates the next, instead of renting growth that stops when spend stops.
content.when_to_use: Growth depends entirely on paid spend and dies when you pause it.
how_to_run: Sketch one loop where a user action (invite, content, data, referral) brings the next user
Invest in strengthening that loop alongside (or instead of) more ad spend
content.failure_modes: A funnel with no loop — growth halts the moment the ad budget does.

items.title: Price off the value, not your costs
triggers: what should i price it at
how do i set my price
what is it worth to them

content.summary: Set price based on what the outcome is worth to the customer, not what it cost you to build or what a competitor charges. Cost is your floor and competitors a reference point, but the value to the buyer is what actually anchors a price.
content.when_to_use: When setting a price for the first time, or when your current price was picked by copying a competitor or marking up costs and you suspect it leaves money on the table.
how_to_run: Write the concrete outcome the customer gets: time saved, money made or saved, risk avoided, a job done they couldn't before. Put a rough number on it per month or use.
Anchor your price as a fraction of that value, leaving the customer clearly ahead, rather than as a markup on your cost.
Sanity-check against the floor (cost to serve must be comfortably below price) and against the buyer's alternatives, including doing nothing.
Talk to a handful of real customers about the value, in their words, before locking the number, and use their language to frame the price.
content.preconditions: You need at least a rough idea of who the customer is and what outcome they're buying, or there's no 'value' to anchor to.
content.failure_modes: Inventing a value number with no customer input and believing it. Pricing off value the customer doesn't perceive or can't measure. Ignoring the cost floor and pricing below what it costs to serve. Assuming all customers value the outcome equally when segments differ. Confusing the value you imagine with what they'll actually pay.
content.evidence_note: Value-based pricing is a long-standing, broadly accepted principle in pricing literature and B2B practice; the directional claim (value beats cost-plus for margin) is well supported. The weak spot is execution: quantifying value is fuzzy and often self-serving, with scarce rigorous studies on small startups. Medium.
content.regional_notes: Perceived value and ability to pay vary sharply by market and currency; the same outcome may justify a far lower price in a lower-income market. Value framing also lands differently in B2B vs consumer.
content.examples: A solo founder priced a scheduling tool at $9/month because it 'felt cheap enough.' The tool saves a small clinic ~6 hours of admin a month; even valued modestly that's worth far more than $9, showing the price was anchored to the founder's comfort, not the buyer's benefit.

items.title: Find out what people will actually pay

content.summary: You can learn willingness to pay by asking structured price questions, but what people say and what they actually pay differ. The most reliable signal is getting someone to commit money or real intent, not answer a survey.
content.when_to_use: Before setting or changing a price when you don't know the range, or to narrow a wide guess before testing for real.
how_to_run: For a quick read, run four price-sensitivity questions on a sample: at what price is this too cheap (you'd doubt quality), a bargain, getting expensive, and too expensive. The overlap suggests an acceptable range.
Treat that range as a hypothesis, not an answer, because stated prices skew low.
Get a revealed signal: put up a real price page and ask people to pre-order, deposit, or sign up to pay, and watch what they do.
Where you can, just charge. Quote a price in a sales conversation or checkout and measure conversion and objections.
Trust revealed behaviour over stated answers wherever they disagree.
content.preconditions: You need enough real prospects (not friends) to ask, and ideally a way to put a real price in front of them.
content.failure_modes: Treating survey answers as truth: stated willingness to pay is systematically lower and noisier than real behaviour, and the four-question method specifically invites lowballing. Asking 'would you pay X' (people say yes to be nice, then never buy). Tiny or biased samples. Asking friends. Confusing 'interest' with 'commitment.' Anchoring respondents with your own number first.
content.evidence_note: The Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter (Peter van Westendorp, 1976) is widely used and well-documented; its four questions and curve-intersection reading are standard. Consensus is it measures stated, not revealed, willingness to pay and biases prices downward. Revealed-preference signals (real purchases, pre-orders) are more reliable. Medium.
content.regional_notes: Price anchors and acceptable ranges differ a lot by country, income, and currency; run the questions per market. Translation of the price questions can also shift answers.
content.examples: A founder surveyed 40 prospects with the four questions and got a range around $15-25/month. When they put up a real checkout at $20, only a couple actually paid while many who'd answered didn't return — a textbook gap between what people said and did.

items.title: Good-better-best and what goes in each tier
triggers: what to put in each plan

content.summary: Offering a few tiers (commonly three) lets different customers self-select and uses the higher tier to anchor the price of the one you most want to sell. Put your core value in the middle tier and reserve scale or premium needs for the top.
content.when_to_use: Once you have a product with more than one type of customer or level of need, and you're designing or revising a pricing page.
how_to_run: List features and sort by who needs them: starters/individuals, your main target customer, and power users or larger teams.
Build three tiers so the middle contains the core value your main customer buys, and make it the one most people choose.
Use the top tier partly as an anchor: a visibly more expensive plan makes the middle look reasonable.
Differentiate tiers by a value metric (usage, seats, outcomes) rather than by withholding basic functionality people need.
Keep it to a few clear tiers; review which tier customers actually pick and adjust.
content.preconditions: You need enough distinct features or usage levels to justify more than one tier; a single simple product may be better as one price.
content.failure_modes: Too many tiers causing choice paralysis. Crippling the cheap tier so badly it poisons trust. Splitting features so the middle feels incomplete. Building tiers around features nobody segments on. A decoy so obviously useless it looks manipulative. Letting the lineup go stale as the product changes.
content.evidence_note: Good-better-best, anchoring with a high-end option, and decoy/compromise effects are well documented in behavioural pricing and consumer-choice research, and widely used in SaaS. Direction is solid; exact tier counts and splits are context-dependent. Medium.
content.regional_notes: The three-tier structure works across markets, but price points per tier should reflect local ability to pay and currency. Some markets are more price-sensitive at the entry tier.
content.examples: A solo founder selling a writing tool had one plan at $12. They split it into a limited starter, a $12 core plan with the features most writers use, and a $39 team plan. Most chose $12, but the visible $39 option made it feel like the sensible choice rather than expensive.

items.title: Free plan, free trial, or paid-only

content.summary: A free plan (freemium) grows reach but most free users never pay; a time-limited trial creates urgency to decide; paid-only filters for serious buyers. Pick based on how fast users reach value and whether free users actually cost you to serve.
content.when_to_use: When deciding how new users first experience the product and how you move them to paying, especially before launch or when free-to-paid conversion is disappointing.
how_to_run: Estimate how quickly a new user reaches a clear 'aha': if fast, a short free trial can work; if it takes weeks of habit-building, a free plan may make more sense.
Estimate what a free user costs to serve and support; if cheap and referral-driving, freemium is more viable.
Choose: freemium for low-cost, viral, habit products; a time-limited trial (often 7-14 days) to force a decision; paid-only when buyers are serious and support is costly.
Define the conversion path: what triggers the upgrade prompt, and what limit (usage, seats, time) pushes a free user to pay.
Track free-to-paid conversion and the cost of the free base; if conversion is far below a few percent and free is expensive, tighten or remove it.
content.preconditions: You need a way to measure activation and conversion; without analytics you can't tell whether free users are a funnel or a sink.
content.failure_modes: A free plan so generous nobody needs to pay. Carrying a large costly free base that never converts and drains support. Trials too short to reach value, or so long they kill urgency. Assuming freemium 'works for everyone' (it suits viral, low-cost products far more than high-touch ones). Confusing sign-ups with a real funnel. Giving away the one feature that's your reason to upgrade.
content.evidence_note: Freemium free-to-paid conversion benchmarks typically land in low single digits (often ~2-5%, varying by category) — the core cautionary fact. The freemium/trial/paid-only trade-offs are widely discussed in SaaS practice. Specific numbers vary, so medium; warn flagged because the 'free users who never convert' trap is common and costly.
content.regional_notes: Free is more expected in some consumer markets and regions; in others a clear price signals seriousness. Payment friction (cards, local methods) strongly affects trial-to-paid by country.
content.examples: A founder launched with a generous free plan and celebrated thousands of sign-ups. A year in, fewer than 2% had ever paid, free-user support ate most of the week, and the free tier already did everything a hobbyist needed. Moving the heaviest features behind a paid plan and adding a 14-day trial lifted paying users without hurting growth much.

items.title: Raising prices without losing customers
triggers: customers will leave if i raise prices

content.summary: Most founders can raise prices more than they fear, especially on new customers. Test increases on new sign-ups first, often grandfather existing customers for a while, and communicate clearly when you change their price.
content.when_to_use: When margins are thin, when you've added real value since setting the price, when you keep hearing 'that's cheap,' or when you suspect you underpriced at launch.
how_to_run: Start with new customers: raise the listed price for new sign-ups and watch whether conversion holds, before touching anyone existing.
If conversion barely moves, you had room; consider a further step. If it drops sharply, you found a ceiling for that segment.
For existing customers, decide whether to grandfather them at the old price for a period or migrate them with clear notice and a value reason.
Communicate increases honestly and in advance, anchored to value delivered, with a window before the change.
Measure revenue and churn after the change; a small loss of customers at a higher price often still increases total revenue.
content.preconditions: You need enough new sign-up volume to read a conversion signal, and a clear record of who is on which price so grandfathering is possible.
content.failure_modes: Never raising out of fear and quietly starving the business. Raising on everyone overnight with no warning, triggering churn and trust damage. Grandfathering forever so old customers anchor the base too low. Judging an increase only by churn and missing that revenue went up. Raising without adding or articulating value. Testing on too small a sample and over-reacting to noise.
content.evidence_note: The pattern that early founders underprice, and that increases on new customers usually move conversion less than feared, is widely reported by pricing practitioners; experience-based, not controlled trials, so medium. Grandfathering and staged testing are standard, low-risk. Warn flagged because mishandled increases (no notice, all at once) genuinely cause churn.
content.examples: A founder hears 'that's cheap' from nearly every customer. They raise the price 60% for new sign-ups only; conversion barely changes and revenue per new customer jumps. Existing customers stay grandfathered for six months, then move to the new price with a month's notice and a note on what's improved.

items.title: Choose what you charge per

content.summary: Your value metric is the unit you charge by (per seat, per usage, per outcome) and it should rise as the customer gets more value. A well-chosen metric means customers who get more pay more, almost automatically.
content.when_to_use: When designing pricing structure, especially before tiers, or when revenue doesn't grow even as heavy customers clearly get more out of the product.
how_to_run: Identify what grows as a customer succeeds with you: more users, more usage, more transactions, more results.
Pick the metric that most closely tracks the value received, is easy to understand and predict, and is hard to game.
Check it scales gently with their growth so the bill feels fair as they expand, not a spike.
Decide between per seat (simple, value is per-person), per usage (aligns with value but can feel unpredictable), or per outcome (strongest alignment, hardest to measure).
Pair the metric with tiers and watch whether small and large customers both feel the price is fair.
content.preconditions: You need to understand how value accrues to the customer over time; without that you can't pick a metric that scales with it.
content.failure_modes: A flat fee so big customers pay the same as tiny ones, leaving money on the table. A metric customers can't predict, so bills shock them and they churn. A metric that's easy to game or that punishes the behaviour you want. Metering on something the customer doesn't connect to value. Changing the metric repeatedly and confusing everyone.
content.evidence_note: The concept and the seat/usage/outcome trade-offs are well established in SaaS pricing practice and widely written about; directional guidance is sound. No single proven-best metric across products; context-dependent. Medium.
content.regional_notes: Usage- and outcome-based billing can be harder where payment infrastructure or budgeting norms favour predictable flat fees; some markets and buyers strongly prefer a fixed, predictable price.
content.examples: A founder charged a flat $30/month for an analytics tool. A one-person blog and a busy media site paid the same despite the site getting 100x the value. Switching to a metric tied to tracked events meant the heavy user's bill grew with usage while the small user paid little and stayed.

items.title: You're probably charging too little
triggers: i feel my price is too low

content.summary: First-time founders almost always underprice, anchoring to their own discomfort rather than the buyer's value. If nobody ever flinches at your price and customers say it's cheap, that's a signal you have room to charge more.
content.when_to_use: When setting an initial price, or whenever you feel nervous about charging and suspect you're leaving money on the table.
how_to_run: Watch for under-pricing signals: almost no one objects, customers say 'that's cheap,' you win deals too easily, or low price makes buyers doubt quality.
Separate your own fear of charging from the customer's actual reaction; the discomfort is usually yours.
Find the ceiling by raising the price for new customers in steps until conversion or objections meaningfully increase, then settle just below.
Use value framing (what the outcome is worth) to justify the higher number to yourself and buyers.
Re-check periodically; as you add value the ceiling moves up.
content.preconditions: You need some sales conversations or sign-ups to observe reactions; with zero customers you have no signal yet.
content.failure_modes: Anchoring price to what you'd personally pay rather than what the buyer values. Reading absence of complaints as 'price is right' when it means 'too low.' Over-correcting into a price the market clearly rejects, then panicking down. Competing only on cheapest, attracting price-sensitive high-churn customers and signalling low quality. Never testing upward.
content.evidence_note: That early founders systematically underprice is one of the most consistent themes among pricing advisors and operators; experience-based pattern, not a controlled study, so medium. The signals (no objections, 'too cheap,' easy wins) are common heuristics. Warn flagged because chronic underpricing quietly kills businesses and reckless over-correction can stall sales.
content.regional_notes: What counts as 'too cheap' or 'too expensive' is strongly tied to local income and currency; test per market rather than porting one price globally.
content.examples: A technical solo founder priced their tool at $5/month because that felt comfortable. Every prospect said yes instantly and several called it 'a steal' — the tell. Raising it to $19 for new sign-ups barely changed conversion and more than tripled revenue, revealing how much room the original price left.

items.title: Landing your first ~10 customers by hand

content.summary: Your first handful of paying customers come from one-to-one effort: you personally find people with the problem, talk to them, and close them by hand rather than expecting a channel to deliver them. The goal is signal and learning, not scale.
content.when_to_use: You have something people can pay for (even rough), zero or near-zero customers, and you're tempted to 'do marketing' before you've sold anything manually.
how_to_run: Write exactly who has the problem badly enough to pay, specific enough to name 30 real people or companies who fit.
Reach those people one at a time through whatever gets a real conversation: a warm intro, a community, a direct message, a call.
Offer to set them up yourself, do onboarding manually, and stay in the conversation so you see every point of friction.
Charge money from the start, even a small amount, so you learn who actually values it versus who's being nice.
After each yes or no, write down why, and adjust who you target and what you say before the next batch.
content.preconditions: Something usable enough that a willing customer can get value, even if you fill gaps manually behind the scenes.
content.failure_modes: Building broad marketing (ads, landing pages, automation) before manually closing anyone, so you never learn why people say no. Giving it away free and mistaking polite signups for demand. Chasing anyone with a pulse instead of a narrow group, producing noisy feedback. Quitting outreach after a few no's when the first ten usually require dozens of conversations.
content.evidence_note: Strong consensus in startup operating literature and accelerator practice that founders should manually recruit early customers before scaling; widely repeated and consistent with how most successful early companies describe first traction. High as practice, though pattern-based rather than controlled study.
content.regional_notes: How you reach the first ten differs by region and segment: in some markets a call or in-person meeting lands far better than email, and messaging apps (WhatsApp, LINE, WeChat) often beat email for direct outreach.
content.examples: A solo technical founder building a scheduling tool for small clinics lists 25 clinics they could reach, messages each personally offering to set it up free that week, gets on three calls, and converts two into paying users they onboard by hand.

items.title: Cold outreach that actually gets replies

content.summary: Cold outreach works when each message is researched, relevant to that specific person, and asks for one small thing; it fails as high-volume identical blasts. Smaller, sharper lists beat bigger generic ones.
content.when_to_use: You have no warm network for a segment you need to reach, and you're deciding between a few dozen tailored messages versus blasting hundreds of identical ones.
how_to_run: Define a tight target (specific role, company type, or situation) so everyone shares the same problem.
For each person, find one real, specific reason you're contacting them and reference it in the first line.
Keep the message short, make the relevance obvious, and end with one easy ask (a reply, a 15-minute call, a yes/no question).
Send in small batches, read every reply, and tighten targeting and wording before the next batch.
Follow up once or twice politely; most replies come on a follow-up.
content.preconditions: A clear enough idea of who has the problem that you can research individuals rather than scrape a giant list.
content.failure_modes: Spray-and-pray: identical messages to huge lists, near-zero replies, and a burned sending reputation. Fake personalization (just merge-tags). Asking for too much too soon (a long demo, a contract). Confusing open rates with interest. Ignoring local spam and consent rules.
content.evidence_note: Vendor benchmark data (millions of emails) consistently shows tightly targeted, genuinely personalized campaigns reply at multiples of broad blasts (e.g. small targeted lists ~5-6% vs ~2% for large sends). Source is self-interested so absolute numbers vary, but the direction is robust and matches first-hand accounts; high on direction, medium on exact figures.
content.regional_notes: Channel and etiquette vary: email dominates in some markets while LinkedIn or local messaging apps work better in others, and several regions have strict anti-spam/consent laws (you may need a lawful basis to contact people). Tone formality also differs by culture.
content.examples: A founder selling a developer tool picks 40 engineering leads who recently posted about the exact pain it solves, sends each a three-sentence note referencing their post and a one-line ask to try it, and gets a handful of replies and two trials — versus an earlier 500-person identical blast that got nothing.

items.title: Mine your warm network before going cold

content.summary: Before cold outreach, work your own and adjacent networks: former colleagues, classmates, past customers, and the people they can introduce you to. Warm intros convert far better and faster than cold for your first customers.
content.when_to_use: You're early and need first conversations fast, and you have any professional or personal network even loosely connected to the problem.
how_to_run: List everyone you know who has the problem or knows people who do, including weak ties you haven't spoken to in years.
Reach out directly and specifically: tell them what you're building and ask either to try it or to introduce you to one or two relevant people.
Make intros effortless by writing a short forwardable blurb they can pass along verbatim.
Treat every warm conversation as both a sales and a research call; ask who else they'd point you to.
Track who you've asked and circle back with progress so the network stays engaged.
content.preconditions: Some existing network, even small or indirect; if you have literally none in the space, lead with communities and cold instead.
content.failure_modes: Never asking because it feels awkward, skipping the highest-converting channel you have. Burning goodwill by hard-selling friends on a bad fit. Asking vaguely ('let me know if you know anyone') instead of naming exactly who you want introduced. Treating the network as the whole plan when it's only enough for the first several customers.
content.evidence_note: Consistent first-hand consensus among early founders and investors that warm intros convert dramatically better than cold; the weak-ties and trust-transfer research supports the mechanism. High, though magnitude is situational and not precisely measured.
content.regional_notes: In many markets and cultures, relationship and referral selling is the dominant norm and cold outreach is mildly taboo, making warm-first even more important; intro etiquette varies widely by region.
content.examples: A first-time founder building an invoicing tool messages a dozen former coworkers now running small businesses; three try it, and two introduce her to other owners in their local business group, producing her first five paying customers without a single cold email.

items.title: Show up where your customers already gather

content.summary: Your customers already congregate somewhere — forums, subreddits, Discords, Slack groups, local meetups. Find those watering holes and become a genuinely useful member; surface your product only when it actually answers a question.
content.when_to_use: You can name where your target users already spend time discussing the problem, and you have patience to contribute before promoting.
how_to_run: List the specific online and offline places your target customers gather around the problem.
Join the few highest-signal ones and read before posting, learning the norms and the real questions.
Contribute real help consistently, building recognition before any mention of your product.
Mention what you built only where it directly answers someone's question, and disclose it's yours.
Concentrate on the communities that produce real conversations and customers.
content.preconditions: Customers who actually congregate somewhere identifiable; very fragmented or offline-only audiences make this weaker.
content.failure_modes: Drive-by self-promotion that gets you muted, banned, or branded a spammer, often poisoning the community for good. Treating it as a place to dump links. Spreading thin across many communities instead of becoming known in a couple. Expecting immediate volume. Mistaking upvotes or thanks for paying demand.
content.evidence_note: Well-established founder practice with many credible first-hand accounts of communities driving early customers; effectiveness is highly community-dependent with little hard benchmark data, plus a real downside risk (bans, reputation), so medium and flagged.
content.regional_notes: Dominant platforms differ sharply by region and segment (Reddit/Discord/Slack in some places; WhatsApp, Telegram, WeChat, LINE, or local forums elsewhere), and offline communities matter more in some markets; each has its own self-promotion norms.
content.examples: A founder building a tool for indie game developers becomes a regular helpful presence in a couple of game-dev Discords for a month, answering technical questions, and when someone asks about exactly the problem the tool solves, mentions it transparently and lands the first several users.

items.title: When content and SEO are worth it early (and when they're too slow)

content.summary: Content and SEO can become a durable, low-cost channel, but on a new site they typically take many months to produce meaningful traffic — too slow to be your path to the first customers. Use them as a compounding bet alongside hands-on selling, not instead of it.
content.when_to_use: You've already validated demand through direct selling, you can sustain output for months, and you want a channel that compounds rather than an immediate-customer source.
how_to_run: Confirm you already have some customers via direct methods before relying on content.
Target specific, low-competition queries your buyers actually search, not broad high-volume terms a new site can't rank for.
Publish genuinely useful content on a cadence you can sustain for at least several months.
Measure leading signals (impressions, rankings on target terms) early and business impact (signups, customers) later.
Decide upfront how long you'll give it, since results lag the work by months.
content.preconditions: Validated demand and capacity to keep producing content for months without immediate return; otherwise it starves attention from faster channels.
content.failure_modes: Treating content/SEO as the way to get the first customers and going quiet when nothing happens for months. Chasing competitive head terms a new domain can't rank for. Publishing thin content nobody searches for. Abandoning it right before results compound. Letting it consume time hand-selling needs more.
content.evidence_note: Multiple SEO-industry sources converge that new sites see early signals ~3-6 months and meaningful business impact ~6-12 months; partly self-interested but consistent, and the slow-to-compound nature is well established. High on the timeline direction; flagged because founders routinely misjudge it as a fast channel.
content.regional_notes: Search behavior and the dominant engine vary by region and language (other engines lead in some markets), and content in less-served languages can face less competition; channel mix shifts where social or video search dominates discovery.
content.examples: A founder with ten paying customers from outreach starts writing a few deeply useful how-to articles a month aimed at niche phrases their buyers search, sees little for a quarter, then gets a steady trickle of qualified signups by months five and six.

items.title: Borrowing someone else's distribution

content.summary: A partner who already has your customers' trust and attention can put you in front of an audience you couldn't reach alone — via integrations, co-marketing, referrals, or reselling. Powerful but slow to set up and rarely worth it before you've proven you can sell directly.
content.when_to_use: You've shown the product converts when you sell it yourself, and there's a non-competing company whose audience is exactly your target and who gains from working with you.
how_to_run: Identify partners who already reach your exact customer and aren't competitors.
Figure out what's genuinely in it for them (revenue, a better product for their users, retention) before pitching.
Start with the lightest possible collaboration (a co-written piece, a referral arrangement, a small integration) to test whether their audience converts.
Make it easy for the partner: do the work, supply the materials, minimize their effort.
Measure customers actually delivered, not vague 'reach,' and only deepen partnerships that produce.
content.preconditions: Proof you can convert customers yourself; partners amplify a working motion but won't fix one that doesn't convert.
content.failure_modes: Spending months negotiating a big partnership before validating the partner's audience converts. Picking partners by logo prestige rather than audience fit. One-sided deals the partner has no reason to push. Depending on a single partner who can change terms or drop you. Mistaking signed agreements for delivered customers.
content.evidence_note: Channel and partnership distribution is a well-documented growth lever, but outcomes vary enormously and many early partnerships underdeliver; little reliable early-stage benchmark data and a real risk of wasted time, so medium and flagged. Operating-practice consensus, not controlled study.
content.regional_notes: Which partners control distribution differs by region (local platforms, telcos, distributors, or marketplaces may be gatekeepers), and partnership/reseller norms and contract expectations vary widely.
content.examples: A founder with a small but converting analytics tool arranges a simple referral and a joint webinar with a non-competing company serving the same shop owners; the first webinar brings a modest but real batch of qualified trials, so they invest in a deeper integration.

items.title: One-off launches: useful spikes, not a channel

content.summary: Launching on a community, marketplace, or via press can produce a real spike of attention and early users, which is genuinely useful for feedback and a few customers. The mistake is treating a one-time spike as a repeatable, sustainable channel.
content.when_to_use: Your product is ready enough to make a strong first impression and you want a concentrated burst of feedback, early users, and signal — while keeping expectations realistic.
how_to_run: Pick one launch surface where your target users actually pay attention, and prepare it properly (clear pitch, assets, a way to capture interested people).
Line up your warm network to engage early so the launch gets initial momentum.
Be present throughout to answer every question and convert attention into conversations.
Capture contact info and feedback from everyone who shows up, since the traffic won't return on its own.
After the spike, evaluate what you learned and which users stuck, and return to your steady channels.
content.preconditions: A product polished enough to withstand a wave of scrutiny and a way to capture the interest the launch generates.
content.failure_modes: Treating a launch spike as proof of a durable channel, then panicking when traffic flatlines. Over-investing in launch theater at the expense of steady distribution. Launching before the product can survive scrutiny, wasting a one-shot moment. Failing to capture visitors. Chasing 'going viral' as a plan rather than a lucky outcome.
content.evidence_note: Many credible first-hand accounts of launches driving a useful one-time burst of users and feedback, and equally many cautioning the spike rarely sustains. Outcomes are highly variable and luck-dependent with no reliable benchmarks, so medium and flagged. Founder-practice consensus.
content.regional_notes: The launch surfaces that matter differ by region and segment (global tech communities and marketplaces in some cases; local press, platforms, or marketplaces in others); press receptivity and which outlets reach your buyers also vary.
content.examples: A founder launches their note-taking tool on a popular community, gets a one-day surge of a few thousand visitors and a few dozen signups plus useful feedback, captures emails from everyone interested, and then goes back to outreach and communities rather than expecting the surge to repeat.

items.title: Should I raise money at all?

content.summary: Most businesses neither need nor suit venture money, and taking it commits you to selling the company or going public. Raise only if you are in a real race where money buys decisive speed, and you genuinely want a high-growth, investor-backed outcome.
content.when_to_use: When you are tempted to raise because it feels like the default next step, or because peers are raising, before you talk to any investor.
how_to_run: Write down what the money is actually for, in one sentence: what does capital let you do that you cannot do with revenue or time?
Ask whether your market rewards speed and scale (winner-takes-most, network effects, land-grab) or whether a steady profitable business is the better outcome.
Sketch both paths honestly: a bootstrapped version and a venture-backed version, and decide which life and which exit you actually want.
If you still lean toward raising, confirm you are prepared for the obligation: outside owners who expect a large exit, not a comfortable steady business.
Default to NOT raising unless the speed-in-a-real-race case is concrete; revisit later if it gets stronger.
content.failure_modes: Raising because it is the cultural default, not because the business needs it; treating a raise as validation or income rather than fuel for a specific plan; underestimating that venture money is a one-way commitment to a big exit, which can kill an otherwise healthy business; confusing a great business with a venture-suitable business (many excellent companies are simply not built for outsized returns).
content.evidence_note: Well-established that only a tiny share of companies raise venture capital (commonly cited well under 1%) and most never take a venture dollar; the broad pattern is solid. The belief that raising is a normal milestone is heavily distorted by survivorship bias, since funded famous companies dominate media while the silent majority are invisible. High for the core claim, lower for any specific percentage.
content.regional_notes: In ecosystems with thin venture markets, raising local institutional money may be impractical, making bootstrapping or revenue the realistic default; in dense hubs, social pressure to raise is stronger and worth resisting deliberately.
content.examples: A solo technical founder builds a niche tool that 300 businesses happily pay for monthly. Raising would force a hunt for a market 100x larger that may not exist; staying bootstrapped lets them keep a profitable business they fully own.

items.title: How much should I raise?
triggers: how much money do i need

content.summary: Raise enough to hit a clear, fundable milestone plus a buffer, not the biggest number you can get. More money means more dilution now and a higher bar to clear at the next round, so a vanity raise can quietly raise your risk.
content.when_to_use: Once you have decided to raise and need to set a target amount before talking to investors.
how_to_run: Define the one milestone this money must buy (e.g. a working product with early paying users, or proof a channel works) that makes the next round fundable.
Build a bottom-up budget: cost the team and spending to hit that milestone over a sensible runway, plus a few months of cushion.
Add a buffer for slippage rather than padding the headline number for ego or optics.
Sense-check the implied dilution: how much of the company you give up, and whether the post-money valuation sets a bar you can realistically beat next time.
Pick the smallest amount that confidently reaches the milestone; you can raise more later from a stronger position.
content.preconditions: You have already decided raising is right and can name the next proof point investors will want.
content.failure_modes: Chasing the largest possible round, maximizing dilution and inflating a valuation you must then outgrow; raising too little and running out before any fundable milestone (a top cause of a painful down round or shutdown); setting the amount by what is offered rather than what the plan needs; ignoring how a high valuation now can trap you if you cannot beat it later.
content.evidence_note: The milestone-plus-buffer logic and ~18-25% dilution per early round are well-supported by current funding data and standard investor practice. Exact numbers vary widely by stage, sector, and region and shift with conditions, so treat any benchmark as a rough anchor. Medium because the principle is robust but the figures are noisy and time-bound.
content.regional_notes: Round sizes, valuations, and what counts as a fundable milestone differ sharply across ecosystems; a number normal in a major hub can be unraisable elsewhere. Calibrate to local comparables.
content.examples: A founder is offered far more than planned. Modeling shows the larger round gives up nearly twice the equity and sets a valuation they'd struggle to double next year, so they take the smaller amount that cleanly funds their next proof point.

items.title: What dilution and the cap table really mean
triggers: how much of my company will i own

content.summary: Every share you issue to investors or employees shrinks your percentage of the company, and that compounds across rounds and option pools. Model your ownership across plausible future rounds and get professional help, because these terms are legally binding and hard to undo.
content.when_to_use: Before signing any investment, option pool, or convertible instrument, and whenever you want to understand where your ownership is heading.
how_to_run: Get a simple cap table listing who owns what, including convertibles or SAFEs that will convert into shares later.
Model forward: estimate your ownership after the next one or two rounds and after any option-pool top-ups, not just today.
Understand option pools are often carved out of founders' share before new money comes in, diluting you more than the headline round suggests.
Have a startup lawyer review the actual terms (not just the price), because details like liquidation preferences and pro-rata rights change who gets what in an exit.
Track the cap table as a living document and update it after every issuance.
content.failure_modes: Focusing only on valuation while ignoring terms that decide outcomes (preferences, anti-dilution, option-pool timing); forgetting SAFEs and notes are real future dilution that lands all at once when they convert; stacking multiple instruments without modeling the combined effect; signing without legal review because the documents looked standard — equity terms are effectively irreversible once executed.
content.evidence_note: Cap-table mechanics, compounding dilution, and the option-pool-before-money convention are standard, well-documented startup finance and broadly agreed — mechanical, not opinion, so high. Survivorship note: founder lore celebrates big raises and glosses over how little equity some celebrated founders kept; don't infer your outcome from theirs.
content.regional_notes: Standard instruments and default terms vary by jurisdiction (the common post-money convertible in some markets vs priced rounds or notes elsewhere), and tax treatment of equity and options differs significantly. Local legal advice is essential.
content.examples: A first-time founder agrees to a seed round and a new option pool without modeling both together. After an earlier SAFE converts and the pool carve-out, they own meaningfully less than assumed — a surprise a one-hour modeling session and a lawyer's review would have prevented.

items.title: Choosing the right investors

content.summary: You will live with your investors for years, so choose for fit, helpfulness, and fair terms, not just the size or speed of the check. Reference-check investors the way they check you, by talking to founders they have backed, including ones whose companies struggled.
content.when_to_use: When you have more than one possible investor, or before accepting any term sheet.
how_to_run: Decide what you actually need beyond money: relevant introductions, domain understanding, patience, or simply staying out of the way.
Ask each investor how they behave when a company hits trouble, then verify it independently.
Take references from founders they have backed, deliberately including companies that did not go well, not just the success stories they offer.
Compare terms and control rights, not only valuation; a friendlier investor with a slightly worse price can be the better deal.
Trust your read on the working relationship; an investor you dread updating is a bad fit even at a great price.
content.preconditions: You have or expect multiple options; with only one offer, weigh taking it against waiting.
content.failure_modes: Optimizing for the highest valuation or fastest close and ignoring fit and behavior under stress; skipping reference checks, or only calling the references the investor selects; mistaking a famous name for useful help; accepting harsh control terms to land a marquee investor.
content.evidence_note: The advice to reference-check investors and weigh fit over headline terms is consensus among experienced founders and operators, but it is judgment and pattern-based rather than statistically proven, and especially prone to survivorship bias (the loudest praise comes from founders whose bets paid off). Medium.
content.regional_notes: In smaller ecosystems the investor pool is limited and reputations travel fast, raising both the value of references and the cost of a bad-fit relationship; in large hubs there are more options but also more hype to see past.
content.examples: A founder has two offers: a higher number from an investor several founders quietly warn is difficult in hard times, and a slightly lower number from one whose backed founders praise their steadiness. They take the lower offer and avoid years of friction.

items.title: Running the raise as a time-boxed process

content.summary: Treat fundraising as a focused, time-boxed sprint with many meetings in parallel, so momentum builds and the raise does not quietly consume the company for months. Running it loosely drains energy from the business and signals weakness to investors.
content.when_to_use: Once you have decided to raise, set a target amount, and are ready to start investor conversations.
how_to_run: Prepare materials and your story first, so you are not iterating the pitch live during the process.
Line up introductions and start conversations in parallel, in a concentrated window, rather than one at a time over months.
Set a target close date and treat the raise as a temporary project with a defined end.
Use parallel timing to create genuine momentum (real interest from several parties) without misrepresenting where you stand.
Protect time for the business; if the raise drags well past the window, pause and reassess whether the timing or story is wrong.
content.preconditions: You have decided to raise and have your target amount and milestone clear.
content.failure_modes: Sequential meetings spread over many months, which kills momentum and stalls the company; manufacturing fake urgency or competing offers, which experienced investors detect and which poisons trust; letting the raise become the founder's full-time job indefinitely while product and customers stall; starting before the story and materials are ready, burning early meetings on a rough pitch.
content.evidence_note: The time-box and parallel-process tactics are widely taught by accelerators and experienced founders and are sensible, but they are heuristics, not proven formulas, and outcomes depend heavily on market conditions and the underlying business. Medium. These tactics are reversible: you can adjust pacing mid-process.
content.regional_notes: Investor decision speed and process norms vary by ecosystem; in slower or relationship-driven markets a strict time-box may be unrealistic and a longer warm-up is normal. Adapt the cadence to local reality.
content.examples: A founder schedules most first meetings within a three-week window instead of trailing them out. Several investors moving at once creates real momentum, the round closes in about six weeks, and the founder returns to building rather than spending half a year pitching.

items.title: Cheaper and non-dilutive alternatives first
triggers: how to fund without giving up equity

content.summary: Before selling equity, exhaust funding that keeps you in control: revenue, customer prepayment or deposits, grants, angels, revenue-based financing, and debt. These are usually cheaper than venture money because you do not give up a permanent share of the company.
content.when_to_use: Whenever you need capital, before defaulting to an equity raise, and especially if your business can fund growth from customers.
how_to_run: Start with revenue: can customers fund the next step through upfront payment, deposits, or annual prepayment in exchange for a discount?
Look for non-dilutive money that fits your context: grants, competitions, or programs you qualify for, which cost time but not equity.
Consider angels or revenue-based financing for smaller, faster capital; RBF typically takes a small share of monthly revenue until a capped multiple is repaid, with no equity given up.
Treat debt carefully: it can be cheaper than equity but must be repaid regardless of how the business does, so size it to what cash flow can cover.
Only move to an equity raise once these cheaper or non-dilutive routes genuinely cannot fund the milestone you need.
content.failure_modes: Defaulting to an equity raise without checking whether customers or non-dilutive sources could fund the same step; taking on debt the business cannot reliably repay (dangerous precisely because it does not flex with bad months); misjudging revenue-based-financing cost (a small revenue share can still be expensive if growth is slow); spending excessive time chasing low-odds grants when revenue would be faster.
content.evidence_note: That revenue, prepayment, grants, angels, RBF, and debt are real and often cheaper alternatives is well-established, and RBF terms (a low single-digit share of revenue, repayment capped around a modest multiple) are documented in current market sources. High for existence and general shape; specific costs and availability vary. Most options here are reversible or self-extinguishing, unlike equity.
content.regional_notes: Availability differs enormously: grants and government-backed programs are generous in some regions and nearly absent in others; revenue-based financing and startup debt are mature in some markets and unavailable in many. Check what genuinely exists where you operate.
content.examples: A founder needs capital to build the next version of a tool. Instead of raising, they offer existing customers a discounted annual prepayment; enough say yes to fund the build, and the founder keeps full ownership and learns which customers are most committed.

items.title: Tie the raise to the next milestone

content.summary: Each raise should buy a specific piece of proof that the next investor will want to see, with enough runway to actually reach it. If you cannot name the milestone the money buys, you are raising blind and risk running out just short of the proof you needed.
content.when_to_use: When sizing a raise, planning spend, or checking whether your current runway will reach a fundable point.
how_to_run: Write down the single most important proof the next investor will demand (e.g. customers will pay and stay, or a growth channel works).
Translate that proof into a dated milestone and the spending plan to reach it.
Calculate runway honestly from real burn, and confirm it comfortably covers reaching the milestone plus a margin, not the bare minimum.
If runway falls short of any fundable milestone, cut burn or raise more before you are desperate.
Re-check monthly: if you are drifting off the path to the milestone, adjust spend early rather than hoping.
content.preconditions: You know roughly what the next funding stage will require as evidence.
content.failure_modes: Raising with no clear milestone, then spending it without reaching anything that unlocks the next round; running out a few months short of the proof point (the classic cause of a forced down round or shutdown); confusing activity (shipping features, hiring) with the specific proof investors want; leaving no margin, so normal slippage tips you into a crisis raise.
content.evidence_note: Linking runway to the next fundable milestone is standard, widely-endorsed startup-finance guidance and logically sound, but what counts as a milestone is judgment-based and market-dependent, and the cautionary stories are shaped by survivorship bias. Medium. The spending and pacing choices are reversible; the raise itself is not.
content.regional_notes: What the next investor expects as proof varies by ecosystem and sector; in markets with less capital, the milestone bar to unlock the next round is often higher and runway needs to stretch further. Calibrate to who your next likely funder actually is.
content.examples: A founder raises a small round but never defines what it must prove. They spend it broadly, and when they go to raise again they have growth but no clean evidence of customer retention — the exact thing investors ask about — so the next raise stalls and cash runs low.

items.title: Scenario Planning
triggers: how do i plan when nothing is certain
what if growth is slower than expected
what if a competitor shows up

content.summary: Instead of betting everything on one forecast, you sketch a few plausible futures (a strong one, a middle one, a weak one) and decide what you'd do in each. It turns a fragile single plan into a set of pre-made responses so surprises don't paralyze you.
content.when_to_use: When the future genuinely hinges on factors you can't predict (funding, adoption speed, a key partner, regulation) and you're tempted to build one optimistic plan. Most useful at the idea/validation stage for setting expectations, and again at scale when committing real money.
how_to_run: Name the 2-3 uncertainties that would most change your outcome (e.g. how fast customers sign up, whether you raise money).
Sketch three coherent futures: a strong case, a likely middle case, and a weak case, each told as a short story not just a number.
For each, write down what you would actually do, what cash you'd need, and what early signal would tell you you're in that world.
Pick the moves that are smart in all three (no-regret moves) and do those now.
Set tripwires: specific numbers or events that tell you which scenario is unfolding so you switch plans early instead of late.
content.preconditions: You need at least a rough sense of your cost structure and the handful of variables that swing your outcome; with zero data the scenarios become pure fiction.
content.failure_modes: Treating the middle case as 'the plan' and ignoring the other two. Writing scenarios so vague they trigger no decisions. Confusing scenarios with forecasts and arguing over which is 'right' instead of preparing for each. Building so many scenarios you analyze forever and act on none. Never setting tripwires, so you don't notice which future you're actually in.
content.evidence_note: Originated in mid-20th-century military and corporate strategy (Herman Kahn at RAND; later large-firm planning teams in the 1970s). Widely taught and credited with faster reaction to shocks, but the famous success stories are retrospective and hard to attribute cleanly. Medium: a sound discipline for thinking under uncertainty, not a predictive tool, with little controlled evidence for tiny startups.
content.regional_notes: Especially valuable for founders in markets with volatile currency, unpredictable regulation, or thin local funding, where the gap between strong and weak cases is wide.
content.examples: A solo founder assumes 100 signups a month and plans to quit her job in six months. Instead she writes three futures: strong (150/mo, quit in 4), middle (60/mo, keep the job and grow part-time), weak (20/mo, the channel fails). Her no-regret move in all three is to test paid ads cheaply this month; her tripwire is under 40 signups in month one means she stays employed.

items.title: Crossing the Chasm
triggers: early users love it but sales stalled
pick a market to focus on
my buyers are too spread out
how to get past the tech enthusiasts

content.summary: The enthusiasts who buy anything new are a different crowd from the practical buyers who only buy proven things, and the jump between them is where many products stall. The fix is to pick one narrow group of practical buyers and become the obvious choice for them before expanding.
content.when_to_use: When early/tech-friendly users adopted happily but mainstream buyers aren't following and growth has flattened, and you're choosing where to concentrate scarce sales effort.
how_to_run: List the distinct groups who could buy, and notice which are pragmatic buyers (they want references and a complete solution, not novelty).
Pick ONE narrow segment small enough to dominate but big enough to matter, where the pain is urgent and buyers talk to each other.
Build the 'whole product' that segment needs to fully solve their problem, not just your core feature.
Win a few visible reference customers there and use their word of mouth as your main engine.
Only after you own that beachhead, expand to adjacent segments that respect those references.
content.preconditions: You already have working early adopters and a product that delivers real value; this is about distribution and focus, not validating whether the product works.
content.failure_modes: Picking a 'niche' that's still everyone. Choosing a segment with no internal word of mouth. Expanding before truly owning the first segment. Mistaking enthusiastic early adopters for mainstream demand. Underinvesting in the surrounding 'whole product' so pragmatic buyers see an incomplete solution.
content.evidence_note: From Geoffrey Moore (1991), building on diffusion-of-innovations research (Rogers). The adoption curve has solid academic roots; the 'chasm/beachhead' prescription is a practitioner framework supported by case studies, not controlled evidence. Medium.
content.regional_notes: The 'whole product' bar varies by region: local-language support, payment methods, and onboarding may be the missing pieces pragmatic buyers demand before switching.
content.examples: A founder's scheduling tool is loved by indie tinkerers but won't grow. They stop selling to 'any small business' and pick independent dental clinics in their city, who share booking headaches and refer each other. They add what those clinics need (local payment, no-show reminders, staff logins), land three references, and let referrals drive the next ten.

items.title: RICE Prioritization
triggers: cant decide what to work on

content.summary: A scoring method to rank competing ideas by how many people they reach, how much they help, how sure you are, and how much effort they cost, so the highest value-for-effort rises to the top. It replaces 'loudest voice wins' with a rough comparable number.
content.when_to_use: When you have more ideas than time and keep flip-flopping. Best once you have some users to estimate reach and impact; less useful before you know your customer.
how_to_run: List the ideas you're choosing between.
Estimate Reach (users/events in a period), Impact (how much it moves what you care about), and Confidence (as a %).
Estimate Effort honestly in person-time (resist lowballing your own).
Compute Reach × Impact × Confidence ÷ Effort and sort high to low.
Use the ranking to start the discussion, then sanity-check the top items against strategy before committing.
content.preconditions: You know your users well enough to make non-random estimates; with no users the numbers are guesses dressed up as math.
content.failure_modes: False precision: a score built on guessed inputs launders a hunch into a 'data-driven' decision. Gaming the effort number for a pet project. Ignoring strategic bets that score low but unlock everything. Over-weighting easy high-reach tweaks while starving differentiating work. Scoring more than building.
content.evidence_note: Created at a product-feedback company (Intercom, ~2016) as an internal practice. A practitioner heuristic with no academic validation; useful as a conversation tool. Low — it organizes judgment, doesn't produce truth, and is only as good as the inputs.
content.examples: A founder weighs five things for next week: a referral feature, faster signup, dark mode, enterprise login, a bug fix. Faster signup reaches every new user, likely lifts activation, takes two days — it tops the list; dark mode reaches everyone but moves no goal, so it sinks despite being fun. They ship the signup fix first.

items.title: Kano Model
triggers: stop adding features no one wants
table stakes vs nice to have

content.summary: Sorts features into three buckets: basics customers assume and only notice when missing, performance features where more is better, and delighters that surprise and please but aren't expected. It stops you polishing delighters while the basics are broken.
content.when_to_use: When deciding which features to build or cut and you suspect you're either missing must-haves or over-investing in extras.
how_to_run: List candidate features.
For each, ask customers two paired questions: how they'd feel if it were present, and if it were absent.
Sort each into basic (absence angers, presence expected), performance (more is better), or delighter (presence pleases, absence fine).
Cover every basic before spending on delighters, then add one or two delighters to stand out.
Re-check over time: today's delighter becomes tomorrow's basic.
content.preconditions: You need access to real prospective customers for the paired questions; doing it from your gut just relabels your opinions.
content.failure_modes: Self-classifying instead of surveying. Chasing delighters while a basic is broken. Treating categories as permanent. Over-engineering performance past what customers care about. Surveying enthusiasts instead of real buyers.
content.evidence_note: Developed by Noriaki Kano (Japan, late 1970s/early 80s) with a published questionnaire method and a real body of follow-up research, though results vary with survey quality. Medium — genuinely useful and reasonably validated, but easy to run sloppily and over-trust.
content.regional_notes: What's a 'basic' vs a 'delighter' differs by market; classify with customers from the region you're selling into.
content.examples: A budgeting-app founder surveys users: bank syncing is a basic (annoying when absent, unimpressive when present), category accuracy is a performance feature (more accurate = happier), a year-end spending recap is a delighter. They fix syncing first, keep improving categorization, and add the recap last as a differentiator.

items.title: Selling the cost of the current-state gap
triggers: they go quiet after the call
not sure why people don't buy
selling features but no one bites
how do i get them to say yes
why is my pitch not landing
they like it but won't pay

content.summary: Instead of pitching features, you diagnose what the buyer's current situation is costing them and make that gap, and its price, concrete. The sale becomes about closing a costly gap rather than buying a tool.
